visits.text: Huangguoshu Waterfall, on the Baishui River, anchors a cluster of 18 cascades spread across 163 km². The main waterfall measures 67 meters high and 83.3 meters wide—the largest single-drop waterfall in Asia. The 17th-century traveler Xu Xiake described it as mist bursting from rock. Behind the curtain of water, Water Curtain Cave extends 134 meters with six natural windows, five chambers, and three springs. On sunny days, a rainbow forms above Rhinoceros Pool.

Doupotang Waterfall, 1 km upstream, is the widest in the group at 105 meters. It served as the filming location for the 1986 television series "Journey to the West," where monk Tang and his disciples cross the river. Before floods, it emits a low rumble that earned it the nickname "roaring waterfall."

Tianxingqiao scenic area, 7 km downstream, covers 4.5 km² of karst landscape where stones, trees, and water form natural compositions. Silver Chain Waterfall falls in parallel threads like a pearl necklace into a turquoise pool.

activities.text: Walk through Gaodang Stone Village, 6 km from the waterfalls: houses, bridges, roofs, and kitchen utensils carved from stone by the Buyi people. The village, founded during the Ming dynasty, forms a circle crossed by a river spanned by a five-arch bridge whose reflection draws a perfect circle.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

places.name: Anyang

visits.text: Yinxu, in Anyang, is one of the world's most significant archaeological sites. It was the last capital of the Shang dynasty, occupied around 1300–1046 BC, rediscovered in 1899 thanks to oracle bones being sold as "dragon bones" to pharmacies. Over 160,000 bone fragments have been unearthed, bearing the earliest known Chinese inscriptions. Inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2006. Budget 3 hours to truly grasp it, ideally with an audio guide.

The Tomb of Fu Hao, within the palace compound, is the only intact Shang royal tomb ever found. Discovered in 1976, it belonged to the consort of King Wu Ding (around 1200 BC). Fu Hao was a queen, priestess, and general: oracle bones record that she led 13,000 soldiers into battle—the largest force ever documented. Nearly 1,928 objects were excavated: 468 bronzes, 755 jades, cowrie-shell currency, and human sacrifices.

The new Yinxu Museum, opened February 26, 2024, is a revelation. 1.8 million visitors in one year. It assembles exceptional Shang bronzes (including a replica of the Houmuwu Ding, the world's largest ancient bronze at 832 kg), original oracle bones, and war chariots. If you see only one thing in Anyang, make it this.

Right next door, the National Museum of Chinese Writing traces the evolution of characters, from oracle bones to Zhou bronzes to the Tang. Of the 4,300 characters identified on oracle bones, 1,600 have been deciphered so far. It's fascinating if you read or are learning Chinese—more arid otherwise.

activities.text: At the Yinxu Museum restaurant, they serve noodles decorated with oracle-bone characters printed in edible squid ink. Tourist gimmick, sure, but it's fun and unexpected after a morning of archaeology.

places.name: Beijing

visits.text: The Forbidden City, built between 1406 and 1420 under Emperor Yongle, sprawls across 178 acres and contains 980 buildings connected by courtyards paved with 12 million bricks. Home to 24 emperors of the Ming and Qing dynasties for five centuries, it now houses the Palace Museum and its vast imperial collections—paintings, bronzes, ceramics, lacquerware. The yellow roofs—a color reserved for the emperor—crown an ensemble that's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987. Start at the Gate of Heavenly Peace, the monumental entrance topped with Mao Zedong's portrait since 1949, then cross through the three great ceremonial halls of the outer court.

Climb Jingshan (Coal Hill), an artificial mound rising 354 feet, built from earth excavated from the Forbidden City's moats. From the summit, you'll get a sweeping view over the entire expanse of golden palace roofs. This is where the last Ming emperor hanged himself in 1644 when an army of 400,000 peasants stormed Beijing.

The Temple of Heaven, built in 1420 under Emperor Yongle and expanded in the 16th century under Jiajing, covers 673 acres—four times the size of the Forbidden City. The Hall of Prayer for Good Harvests, a 125-foot-tall rotunda with a 98-foot diameter, rests on 28 wooden pillars assembled without a single nail or drop of cement. The four central pillars symbolize the seasons, the next twelve represent the months. The emperor came here twice a year to pray to Heaven for bountiful harvests. Struck by lightning in 1889, the hall was rebuilt identically the following year. It's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998.

Step into the hutongs of the Nanluoguxiang quarter, one of Beijing's oldest alleyways with over 800 years of history. These narrow passages date back to the Yuan dynasty (1271-1368)—the word 'hutong' likely comes from the Mongolian 'hottog,' meaning 'well.' The courtyard houses (siheyuan) are arranged around small courtyards planted with pomegranate trees, a symbol of fertility. Beijing had 3,216 of them in 1944; fewer than 1,000 remain today.

The Mutianyu section of the Great Wall, 45 miles from Beijing, was first built in the mid-6th century under the Northern Qi dynasty, then reconstructed in granite in 1569 by General Qi Jiguang during the Ming period. The restored stretch runs 7,380 feet and features 22 watchtowers—an unusual density, with a tower roughly every 330 feet. The walls reach 23 to 26 feet in height and 13 to 16 feet in width at the top. Unique feature: crenellations line both sides of the walkway, which is exceptional—on most other sections, they only protect the outer side. The Mutianyu Pass, with its three interconnected watchtowers (Zhengguantai), forms a defensive structure found nowhere else on the Wall.

The Summer Palace, designed in 1750 by Emperor Qianlong to celebrate his mother's 60th birthday, covers 716 acres—three-quarters of which are occupied by Kunming Lake. The emperor had 100,000 workers excavate and enlarge the lake, using the earth to raise Longevity Hill. Destroyed in 1860 by Franco-British troops during the Second Opium War, it was rebuilt in 1886 by Empress Cixi—who diverted funds meant for naval modernization. The Long Corridor, a covered gallery stretching 2,388 feet, is adorned with thousands of paintings on wood depicting landscapes, flowers, and scenes from Chinese literature. At the lake's edge, Cixi's marble boat, 118 feet long, remains moored on the north shore. It's been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1998.

The 798 Art District occupies a former industrial complex of 6.9 million square feet built in the 1950s by East German architects in Bauhaus style. Factory 718, intended for manufacturing military electronic components, was divided into six units in the 1960s—including Unit 798, which gave the district its name. After subsidies dried up in the early 1990s, the large workshops with high ceilings and north-facing skylights attracted artists drawn by cheap rents. Today there are over 200 galleries, including UCCA (107,000 square feet of exhibition space) and Tang Contemporary Art.

activities.text: Watch the changing of the guard at Tiananmen Gate and cross the world's largest public square (109 acres).
Rise early to watch local residents at the Temple of Heaven Park practicing tai chi, dancing, singing, and playing cards beneath centuries-old cypresses—an unchanging morning ritual.
Take a rickshaw ride through the Shichahai hutongs, Beijing's largest protected quarter, winding between lakes, weeping willows, and Qing dynasty mansions.

Take the cable car up to Tower 14, then hike the ramparts between Towers 14 and 20 (western section) with panoramic views over forested hills—vegetation covers over 96% of the area. You can descend by toboggan from Tower 6, a slide that winds through the forest.

Cross the Seventeen-Arch Bridge, built in 1750 under Qianlong: 490 feet long, it links the lakeshore to Nanhu Island and bears over 500 carved stone lions, each one different. From the bridge's midpoint, you can count nine arches on each side—the number nine symbolized imperial power.

visits.text: Chaka Salt Lake ("salt lake" in Tibetan), at 3,059 meters elevation, covers 105 km²—ten times the area of Hangzhou's West Lake. Its salt layer reaches 5 meters deep in places. In calm weather, the lake's surface reflects the sky and snowy Qilian Mountains like a perfect mirror, earning it the nickname "Mirror of the Sky." National Tourism Geographic Magazine ranked it among the "55 places to see in a lifetime."

The old salt railway tracks, built in the 1950s to transport production, extend into the lake. Now abandoned, they serve as a photographic promenade. A tourist train runs between different areas of the site. Salt exploitation in the region dates back over 3,000 years, to the era of the Qiang people.

activities.text: Photography at sunrise or sunset on Chaka Lake—the best times for the mirror effect are before 9am and after 5pm, when the wind is low and light rakes across the surface. Wear brightly colored clothing (red, white, yellow) for reflections. Don't walk barefoot on the salt crust (cuts).

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

places.name: Chengdu

visits.text: The Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding, founded in 1987 from six rescued, sick and starving pandas, now spans 3 km² of forested hills and bamboo groves just 10 km from downtown. It's home to the world's largest captive giant panda population—over 200 individuals living in enclosures that recreate their natural habitat. Arrive early: pandas are most active during morning feeding and nap through the afternoon. The nursery lets you watch newborns in incubators (best in autumn-winter). The Giant Panda Museum, opened in 1993, displays more than 800 documents, fossils and models—it's the world's only museum entirely dedicated to this species.

Wuhou Temple (Wǔhóu Cí), originally built in 223 CE, is the only temple in China that jointly honors an emperor and his prime minister: Liu Bei, founder of the Shu Kingdom, and Zhuge Liang, the legendary strategist of the Three Kingdoms period (220-280). The current complex, rebuilt in 1672 under Emperor Kangxi, covers 37,000 m². Liu Bei Hall houses his 3-meter gilt statue flanked by his generals Guan Yu and Zhang Fei. The Stele of Three Perfections, erected in 809, stands 3.67 m tall—it combines text by Minister Pei Du, calligraphy by Liu Gongquan, and a eulogy to Zhuge Liang. Behind the temple, the Huiling Mausoleum (Liu Bei's tomb) has remained intact for over 1,700 years, never violated or excavated.

Jinli Ancient Street, adjacent to Wuhou Temple, recreates Qing Dynasty commercial architecture across roughly 550 meters. Its wooden houses with gray tiles and red eaves shelter shops selling Sichuan handicrafts (Shu embroidery, shadow puppets, Sichuan opera masks) and street stalls serving local classics: dan dan noodles (sesame noodles with chili oil), mapo tofu, and mala skewers. After dark, red lanterns light up and the atmosphere transforms—this is when to try san da pao, glutinous rice balls launched against a metal plate that bounce into soybean flour.

The Kuanzhai Alleys (Kuānzhǎi Xiàngzi), right in downtown Chengdu, are the last remnants of the Manchu city of Shaocheng, built in 1718 under Emperor Kangxi to house soldiers of the Eight Banners after the campaign against the Dzungars in Tibet. Of the original 42 lanes, only three survive: Wide Lane (Kuan, 7-8 m wide, reserved for officers who traveled by carriage), Narrow Lane (Zhai, 5 m, a Sino-Western mix tied to late-Qing foreign residents), and Well Lane (Jing, named after a well dug under Kangxi). The 45 interior courtyards (sìhéyuàn) were restored between 2004 and 2008. Here you sip covered-bowl tea (gàiwǎn chá) from bamboo chairs while watching Sichuan opera face-changing performances (biànliǎn).

People's Park (Rénmín Gōngyuán), a few minutes from Kuanzhai Alleys, is the heart of everyday Chengdu life. Under bamboo and ginkgo trees, retirees practice tai chi, play cards and mahjong, dance in couples, or fly kites. Open-air teahouses set up tables along the artificial lake—order jasmine tea and watch the ballet of hot-water pourers with their long-spouted kettles. On Sundays, an improvised "marriage corner" sees parents display profiles of their single children on placards.

Qingyang Palace (Qīngyáng Gōng), 1.2 km from People's Park, is one of Sichuan's oldest and most important Taoist temples. Founded during the Tang Dynasty, it takes its name from two bronze goats (qīngyáng) placed before the main hall—tradition says touching them brings good luck. The main building, the Hall of Three Purities (Sānqīng Diàn), is a nail-free wooden structure supported by massive columns.

The Leshan Giant Buddha (Lèshān Dàfó), 126 km south of Chengdu, is the world's tallest stone Buddha statue: 71 meters high, carved into the red sandstone cliff of Mount Lingyun at the confluence of the Min, Dadu, and Qingyi rivers. Construction began in 713 during the Tang Dynasty, initiated by monk Haitong who wanted to calm the dangerous waters of the confluence. When funds were threatened by corrupt officials, Haitong gouged out his own eyes to prove his sincerity—the extortionists fled. The monk died before completion; his disciples continued the work for 90 years until 803. Rock debris thrown into the river during construction actually altered the currents, making navigation safer. The head measures 14.7 m high, the ears 7 m, the fingers 8.3 m, and the feet can accommodate a hundred people. The hair consists of 1,021 spiral buns, each individually set into the rock. Descend via the Nine Bends Plank Road (Jiǔqū Zhàndào), a 278-step path carved into the cliff that leads from the top of the head to the feet of the colossus. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1996.

Mount Qingcheng (Qīngchéng Shān), 68 km west of Chengdu, is one of the birthplaces of Taoism in China. This is where Zhang Daoling supposedly founded the Taoist School of Five Pecks of Rice in the 2nd century CE. The main path climbs through dense forest of cedars and bamboo, connecting about ten Taoist temples and pavilions up to the 1,260-meter summit. A cable car can shorten the ascent. Jianfu Temple at the mountain's base dates to the Tang Dynasty (built in 730). The site is a UNESCO World Heritage Site along with the Dujiangyan Irrigation System.

The Dujiangyan Irrigation System (Dūjiāngyàn), 3 km from the town of the same name, is the world's oldest irrigation system still in operation. Built in 256 BCE by Governor Li Bing and his son under the State of Qin, it diverts the waters of the Min River without a dam, through three complementary structures: a levee (Yuzui) that divides the current, a diversion channel (Baopingkou) tunneled through the mountain, and a spillway (Feishayan) that regulates floods. This system still irrigates over 5,300 km² of agricultural plains today and enabled Sichuan to become China's "rice basket." UNESCO listed in 2000.

activities.text: Sichuan cooking class in a downtown workshop. Learn to balance Sichuan spices—numbing Sichuan pepper (huājiāo) that tingles the tongue, dried chili, and fermented broad bean paste (dòubàn jiàng) from Pixian. Dishes taught vary: kung pao chicken, mapo tofu, or steamed dumplings. Allow 2 to 3 hours, tasting included.

Hike Mount Qingcheng via the main trail (ascent about 2h, descent 1h30). The path crosses suspension bridges, tunnels carved into rock, and clearings where Taoist monks sell green tea grown on the slopes. Option to shorten via cable car and ferry across Yuecheng Lake.

visits.text: Hongyadong (Hóngyádòng), clinging to the cliff overlooking the Jialing River, is an 11-story, 75-meter-high complex built in the style of traditional stilt houses (diàojiǎolóu) of Bayu culture. The site dates back over 2,300 years—it was originally a fortified gate and major commercial wharf on the river. The current complex, completed in 2006, reproduces this characteristic wooden stilt architecture of southwestern China, adapted to mountainous terrain and floods. Peculiarity: the 1st floor and the 11th floor each open onto different streets at different elevations. In the evening, red lanterns illuminate the structure and its reflection in the river—an image often compared to the spirit world in the film Spirited Away.

Ciqikou (Cíqìkǒu), "the porcelain port," is a town over 1,000 years old on the bank of the Jialing River in Shapingba District. During the Ming and Qing dynasties, it was a center of porcelain production and trade. Its cobbled lanes bordered by two-story wooden houses preserve the atmosphere of an old riverside town. You'll find hand-pulled caramel candy workshops (máhuā), traditional teahouses, and handicraft shops. On weekends, artists play traditional Sichuan music in interior courtyards.

Liziba (Lǐzǐbà) monorail station became famous for a simple reason: Line 2 of the monorail passes through a 19-story residential building between the 6th and 8th floors. This isn't an accident—the building was designed simultaneously with the line, opened in 2005, to adapt to Chongqing's extreme topography, a city built on steep hills between two rivers. The monorail enters and exits the building every few minutes with surprisingly little noise thanks to soundproofing systems.

The Yangtze River Cableway (Chángjiāng Suǒdào), opened in 1987, is the only urban cableway still in service crossing the Yangtze. The cabin soars over the river for about 1,166 meters, linking Yuzhong District to the south bank (Nan'an). The crossing takes 4 minutes and offers a bird's-eye view of the river, bridges, and Chongqing's skyline. It's a relic from the era when Chongqing, "the mountain city," used cableways as daily public transport before the metro was built.

Chaotianmen Dock (Cháotiānmén), at the tip of the Yuzhong Peninsula, marks the exact confluence of the Yangtze and Jialing rivers. This is the wharf where Three Gorges cruises depart. The square offers a panoramic view of the confluence where the Jialing's green waters meet the Yangtze's brown waters—the demarcation line is visible in clear weather. At night, illuminated boats and Jiangbei's skyscrapers compose Chongqing's nocturnal panorama.

activities.text: Chongqing hot pot (huǒguō) tasting, the city's absolute specialty. The bright red broth, saturated with chili, Sichuan pepper, and beef tallow, boils in a pot at the center of the table. Each diner plunges thin meats, tripe, vegetables, and tofu into it. The local version is reputed spicier and fattier than Chengdu's. In the Jiefangbei or Nanbin Lu districts, specialized restaurants serve until late into the night.

Late afternoon or evening boarding at Chaotianmen wharf for the Three Gorges cruise toward Yichang (4 days / 3 nights downstream). The ship typically casts off around 9 PM. From the upper deck, Chongqing's nighttime skyline scrolls past as the vessel enters the Yangtze.

days_plan.itin_title: Southwest China: Sichuan, Chongqing & the Yangtze Gorges
Southwest China: Sichuan, Chongqing & the Yangtze Gorges

visits.text: Daocheng (稻城, 3,800m) is the gateway town for Yading—a transit town that merits half a day. Bangpu Monastery, built in 1144 by the 1st Karmapa, Düsum Khyenpa, is the region's oldest monastery. It's 30km from town, at 3,940m altitude, amid barren hills. The building itself is damaged, half-restored, but its location facing a deep valley is striking.

Daocheng's center has its decorated-facade Tibetan houses, butchers selling yak, and grocery stores at the main crossroads. 96% of the county's population is Tibetan. Stroll the town streets: it's the last real town before Yading and the next stage—buy snacks, hot water bottles, and chocolate bars for tomorrow's hikes.

Daocheng Yading Airport (4,411m altitude, the world's highest commercial airport) is 50km from town. If you wish, a detour to the landing strip gives a sense of the project's magnificent absurdity: A321s landing in the clouds amid sacred mountains.

activities.text: Purchase Yading Reserve entrance tickets in advance—the online ticket office is often faster. The ticket includes multi-entry access and internal tourist buses. Also arrange with your guide for accommodation in Riwa village (at the reserve entrance), far more practical than returning to Daocheng each evening.
Sunset over Daocheng hills from the town heights—head west in late afternoon. The golden grasslands and snow-capped ridges form a panorama typical of southern Kham.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: High-speed train Beijing→Datong (~2h). The Yungang Grottoes, 16 km from Datong: 252 caves and 51,000 Buddhist statues carved into sandstone between 460 and 525 under the Northern Wei. The complex stretches 1 km along the cliff face of Mount Wuzhou. The five Tan Yao caves (nos. 16-20) each shelter a colossal Buddha representing a Tuoba emperor—an act of repentance following anti-Buddhist persecutions. Cave no. 20: 13.7 m Buddha in meditation. Cave no. 5: largest statue at the site (17 m). The style blends Persian, Greco-Buddhist Gandharan, Indian and Chinese influences. UNESCO since 2001.
Caves nos. 5 and 6, the best preserved. Cave no. 6 features a central pagoda adorned with scenes from the life of Buddha. After the capital relocated to Luoyang in 494, work gradually ceased.

The Hanging Monastery Xuankong, 65 km from Datong, built in 491 under the Northern Wei. Plastered 50 m high on the Jinlong canyon cliff face, opposite Mount Hengshan (sacred Taoist mountain). The structure is held by horizontal beams driven into the rock—the red wooden pillars are purely aesthetic. Forty pavilions linked by vertiginous walkways, 80 statues. The only monastery in China honoring Buddhism, Taoism and Confucianism—Sakyamuni, Lao Tzu and Confucius side by side.

The Nine Dragons Screen of Datong (1392), in front of the palace of Emperor Ming Hongwu's thirteenth son. Measuring 45.5 m long and 8 m high, covered in glazed tiles. The oldest and largest of China's three existing Nine Dragons screens.

activities.text: Ascent into the monastery via stone stairway carved into the cliff, crossing the Changxian bridge between the north and south sections—plunging view over the canyon.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

visits.text: The Shaolin Temple was founded in 495 under Emperor Xiaowen of the Northern Wei to welcome the Indian monk Batuo. In 527, Bodhidharma arrived, reputedly meditating nine years facing a cliff in a cave on Mount Song and founding Chan (Zen) Buddhism along with the foundations of Shaolin martial arts. Inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2010, the complex covers 57,600 m² but remains highly touristic: arrive at opening (8 am) to visit before the tour buses.

300 meters west of the main temple, the Pagoda Forest (Talin) gathers over 240 funerary stupas of eminent monks, dating from the 8th century (Tang) to the 19th (Qing). It's China's largest pagoda cemetery, peaceful and photogenic. Each stupa tells the life of the interred monk: the number of tiers reflects his rank, calligraphy adorns the base.

High up at 1,360 meters elevation, the Damo (Bodhidharma) Cave must be earned: 30 minutes on foot or 5 minutes by cable car from Shifang Temple. The cave is small, empty, but this is where it all began, according to tradition. The view over the Shaoshi Valley is splendid on clear days.

Songyang Academy, at the southern foot of Mount Song, is one of the four great Confucian academies of the Song dynasty. Founded in 484 under the Northern Wei, it hosted the brothers Cheng Hao and Cheng Yi, founders of Neo-Confucianism. But its true stars are two giant cypress trees: according to tradition, they were named "Great Generals" by Emperor Han Wudi himself over 2,000 years ago—among the oldest trees in China.

Zhongyue Temple, 4 km east of Dengfeng, is the largest and best-preserved of the five imperial temples dedicated to sacred mountains. Rebuilt mainly under the Qing, it retains its original Han layout with a succession of 11 courtyards spanning nearly a kilometer. It's a birthplace of Chinese Taoism, still vibrant: the monks practice here.

The Gaocheng Astronomical Observatory, 15 km southeast of Dengfeng, is China's oldest preserved observatory. Built in 1276 under the Yuan by astronomer Guo Shoujing on Kublai Khan's orders, it was used to measure the shadow of the gnomon: standing 12.62 meters tall, it was one of 27 observatories in the imperial network. The Shoushi calendar computed here remained in use until the end of the Ming.

On the way, make a quick stop at Songyue Pagoda (520), the oldest brick pagoda in China. Twelve sides, fifteen tiers, 40 meters tall, in the middle of the forest. It's stood for 1,500 years. Many miss it because it's off the standard circuits.

activities.text: Watch a kung fu demonstration at the Shaolin Wushu Training Center, right at the complex entrance. Three performances daily (10 am, 11:30 am, 2 pm), 30 minutes each. The techniques are choreographed for tourists, but the young monk-students (ages 8–15) are genuinely talented. Avoid weekends and Chinese holidays if you want to see anything.

For those who want to go deeper, several schools in the Dengfeng valley (Tagou, Shaolin Epo) offer 1- to 3-hour kung fu introductory classes with a senior monk, for around 200–400 yuan. It's touristic but sincere, and far more instructive than the performances.

If you're up for a hike, climb Junji Peak on Mount Song (1,491 m), starting from Zhongyue Temple on foot. Count on 4 to 5 hours round-trip, marked trail, quiet on weekdays. It's one of the five sacred Taoist mountains, and the least known: you'll cross paths with Chinese pilgrims rather than foreign tourists.

days_plan.itin_title: Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

visits.text: The Mogao Caves (Mògāo Kū), 25 km southeast of Dunhuang, constitute the world's largest, richest, and oldest Buddhist cave art complex. Carved starting in 366 AD—when the monk Le Zun had a vision of a thousand Buddhas bathed in golden light on the cliff face—the caves were expanded over ten centuries until the 14th century. 492 painted caves survive, containing 45,000 m² of murals and over 2,000 polychrome sculptures. Cave 96, the most spectacular, holds a 35.5-meter-tall Buddha. Cave 17, called the "Library Cave," discovered in 1900 by a Taoist monk, contained over 50,000 manuscripts, silk paintings, and textiles sealed for nearly 1,000 years—the greatest medieval East Asian document discovery. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987. Advance booking required.

The Singing Sand Dunes of Mingsha (Míngshā Shān), along the southern edge of the Dunhuang oasis, stretch 40 km long and rise over 250 meters high. Their name comes from the deep rumble the sand produces when it slides down slopes in dry weather—an acoustic phenomenon linked to the particular grain size of the sand particles. At the dunes' foot, Crescent Moon Lake (Yuèyá Quán) is a natural crescent-shaped spring 218 meters long and about 50 meters wide, fed by an underground water table that prevents sand from covering it. Historical texts mention this spring since the Han dynasty (2nd century BC).

Yumenguan Pass (Yùmén Guān), 90 km northwest of Dunhuang, was one of two gateways out of the Chinese empire toward the western routes. Its name, "Jade Gate Pass," comes from jade from Khotan that passed through this post. Only a square rammed-earth fort remains, 24 meters per side and 10 meters high, isolated in the Gobi Desert. Tang dynasty poets made it a symbol of frontier garrison solitude.

activities.text: Ride a camel across the Mingsha sand dunes as Silk Road merchants did for millennia. The singing sands produce an eerie humming sound when grains shift in the wind.
Camel ride around the Crescent Moon Lake oasis.
Free evening to watch sunset over the dunes.

Camel ride through the Mingsha dunes at sunset. Camel caravans follow a marked circuit from the dunes' base up toward the ridges. The descent can be done on sand sleds. In the evening, the dunes take on orange then purple hues.

visits.text: Drive to Fenghuang (~4h). Old town ('Phoenix'), built under the Tang along the Tuojiang River, inhabited by the Miao and Tujia. Stilt houses (diaojiaolou) in wood and bamboo assembled without nails or bolts. Ming ramparts.
Hongqiao Bridge (red lanterns, Miao shops), North Gate tower, Wanming Pagoda.

activities.text: Boat on the Tuojiang River at sunset—lanterns lit, reflections of stilt houses on the water.
Crossing on the stepping stones between the two banks.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

visits.text: Reed Flute Cave (Lúdíyán), 5 km northwest of downtown, takes its name from the reeds that once grew at its entrance—perfect for making bamboo flutes. Carved by karst erosion roughly 700,000 years ago, the cave extends 240 meters deep with 500 meters of galleries open to visitors. Seventy-seven ink inscriptions on the walls date back to the Tang dynasty (618–907), making this one of China's oldest documented tourist sites. The central hall, the Crystal Palace of the Dragon King, measures 93 meters wide and 18 meters high. During World War II, over a thousand Guilin residents sheltered here during Japanese bombing raids.

Elephant Trunk Hill (Xiàngbíshān), where the Taohua River meets the Li River, is Guilin's emblem. This 55-meter limestone pinnacle, formed 360 million years ago on an ancient seabed, depicts an elephant dipping its trunk into the water. Between trunk and body opens the Shuiyue Cave (Moon in the Water), 12 meters high and 9.5 meters wide—at night, its arch and reflection form a perfect circle on the river. At the summit stands Puxian Pagoda, built during the Ming dynasty (1368–1644), rising about 14 meters. Legend tells of a celestial elephant sent by the Jade Emperor who fell in love with Guilin and refused to return; enraged, the Emperor pierced it with a sword, transforming it into stone.

Solitary Beauty Peak (Dúxiù Fēng) and the Jingjiang Princes' Palace occupy the exact center of Guilin. The palace was built between 1372 and 1392 for Zhu Shouqian, great-nephew of the Ming dynasty founder—its construction predates Beijing's Forbidden City. Fourteen princes across twelve generations lived here for 257 years. Under the Qing, the palace became an imperial examination center where over 590 scholars trained. The peak itself—a 66-meter limestone pillar formed 350 million years ago—bears 136 rock inscriptions on its flanks, the most famous carved over 800 years ago: "Guilin's scenery is the finest under heaven." Climb 306 steps to the summit for a 360° view over the city and Li River.

Seven Star Park (Qīxīng Gōngyuán), Guilin's largest at 120 hectares, takes its name from seven karst peaks arranged like the Big Dipper. A destination since the Sui and Tang dynasties (6th–10th century), it shelters the Seven Star Cave, whose galleries stretch over 1.5 km through Putuo Mountain—an ancient underground river roughly one million years old. Entry is through the Flower Bridge (Huā Qiáo), Guilin's oldest bridge, built during the Song dynasty (960–1279). At the foot of Crescent Mountain, the Guihai Forest of Steles assembles 218 engraved tablets from the Tang through Qing dynasties, considered China's second-largest stele collection after Xi'an.

Daxu old town (Dàxū), 15 km southeast of Guilin on the Li River's banks, is one of Guangxi's four best-preserved ancient towns. Founded in 200 BCE, it was an important trading post on the Li River. Its lanes paved with river pebbles are lined with wooden and stone houses from the Ming and Qing dynasties. The covered market, still active, sells fermented tofu, medicinal herbs, and local basketwork.

activities.text: Evening stroll along the Two Rivers and Four Lakes circuit (Liǎng Jiāng Sì Hú). The Sun and Moon Pagodas, rising from Shanhu Lake, light up after dark—the Sun Pagoda, cast in bronze, stands 41 meters tall across 9 stories. The route follows willow-lined and banyan-shaded banks, offering views of illuminated karst peaks in the heart of the city.

Tasting Guilin rice noodles (Guìlín mǐfěn) at a downtown shop. The local recipe uses a pork and beef broth slowly simmered with spices, served over fresh rice noodles with braised meat and pickled vegetables. Every neighborhood has its variant—try the smoked horse version (mǎròu mǐfěn), a local specialty since the Qin dynasty.

Final shopping and tasting at Guilin's central market: Guangxi tea, Flower Bridge hot pepper sauce, osmanthus cakes (guìhuā gāo)—the osmanthus flower is Guilin's emblem, whose name literally means "osmanthus forest."

days_plan.itin_title: Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces

visits.text: The walled town of Qingyan, 29 km south of Guiyang, was built in 1378 as a Ming military outpost to control the empire's southwestern frontier. Constructed entirely from local limestone—walls, streets, houses—it sprawls across 3 km² and preserves four monumental gates rising 9.5 meters high. A rare feature in China: Buddhism, Taoism, Catholicism, and Protestantism have coexisted within its walls since a French missionary arrived in 1851.

Step through the gate of Ciyun Temple in Qingyan, where stone carvings on column bases rank among the finest in Guiyang. Wanshou Palace, a former Jiangxi merchant's trading house converted into a Taoist temple in 1778, preserves a theater stage adorned with bas-reliefs depicting historical legends.

Jiaxiu Tower, in central Guiyang, stands on a rock in the middle of the Nanming River. This three-story Ming dynasty wooden structure with upturned eaves served as a study and meditation retreat for scholars. It remains the city's most recognizable symbol.

activities.text: Street food tasting in Qingyan's alleyways: braised pork trotters in soy sauce (a local specialty since the Qing dynasty), Qingyan tofu, and rice cake porridge rank among Guizhou's most celebrated snacks.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: En route to Gyantse, Kamba La Pass (4,794m) offers the first view of Yamdrok Lake (羊卓雍错)—a 630km² sacred turquoise lake encircled by snow-capped mountains. It's one of Tibet's three great sacred lakes along with Namtso and Manasarovar. Stop for 20 minutes for photos from the pass.

Pelkor Chode Monastery (白居寺, 3,900m) is unique in Tibet: it unites under one roof the three major Tibetan Buddhism schools—Gelugpa, Sakyapa, and Kadampa—a feat of inter-sect tolerance founded in 1418 by Prince Rabten Kunzang Phak. Its assembly hall houses an 8-meter bronze Sakyamuni statue. The adjoining Kumbum is its masterpiece.

Gyantse's Kumbum (十万佛塔) is a 32-meter stupa built between 1418 and 1427, the only stupa of this type in all of Tibet. Its 9 stories house 77 chapels, 108 doors, and more than 10,000 Buddha images. Climb chapel by chapel clockwise: the 15th-century frescoes, in Newar-Tibetan style, are among the finest in all Himalayan Buddhist art. On the 4th floor, four pairs of eyes watch the four cardinal directions.

activities.text: Ascend Gyantse Dzong (14th-century medieval fortress) dominating the town from a rocky spur. In 1904, Younghusband's troops bombarded it during the British expedition to Tibet. The museum inside relates this colonial chapter from the Tibetan viewpoint. Splendid view over the town and valley.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: West Lake (西湖) has been UNESCO-listed since 2011 - one of the rare designed landscapes to receive this recognition. It covers 6.39 km², punctuated by three artificial islands built during the Song Dynasty (960-1279). The Bai Causeway, 1.5 km long, bears the name of the Tang poet who had the lake dredged when he was magistrate of Hangzhou in the 9th century. Marco Polo described the city as "the most beautiful in the world."

Leifeng Pagoda (雷峰塔) rises on the lake's south shore, rebuilt in 2002 as an identical replica of the Song original (977 CE) that collapsed in 1924. The new tower incorporates the ancient foundations, visible on the ground floor through a glass floor. From the top, the view over the lake and Su Causeway is Hangzhou's finest.

Baochu Hill (宝叔岭) north of the lake offers the most classic climb - 45 minutes from the water's edge to Baochu Pagoda (renovated in 1933). From the summit, the entire lake spreads beneath your feet: the five causeways, the islands, and the hills on the opposite shore.

Lingyin Temple (灵隐寺), founded in 326 CE by Indian monk Huili, is one of China's ten largest Buddhist temples - reconstructed sixteen times. The adjacent Feilai Peak (飞来峰, Peak that Flew Here) is covered with 470 rock carvings made between the 10th and 14th centuries, including a particularly remarkable bas-relief Laughing Buddha.

If you leave early this morning, the Longjing tea plantations (龙井) 4 km west of the lake await you. Longjing (Dragon Well) is China's most prized green tea - leaves hand-flattened in cast-iron woks. In April, it's Mingqian season, the first harvest before the Qingming Festival, the finest and most expensive.

Su Causeway (苏堤), 2.8 km long, crosses the lake from shore to shore with six arched bridges and weeping willows. Built under the governance of poet Su Dongpo in the 11th century with silt from dredging the lake, it's inscribed within the UNESCO perimeter. The late afternoon walk is one of Hangzhou's most pleasant.

activities.text: Rent a bike and do the full West Lake loop (15 km). The bike paths are flat, well-marked, follow the water almost everywhere. It's the best way to see the lake without getting stuck with walking groups.

Dine on the lake shores in the Nanshan Road area. Try: Dongpo pork (pork braised in rice wine, recipe attributed to poet Su Dongpo, magistrate here in the 11th century), West Lake vinegar fish, and cooked lotus.

Longjing tea tasting at a producer's in Longjing or Meijiawu villages. Several teahouses set among the tea bushes offer guided tastings with views over the cultivation rows. Much cheaper than in the city.

Take a boat ride on the lake to reach Three Pools Mirroring the Moon Island (三潭印月) - three small stone towers built in the water during the Song Dynasty. The crossing takes 20 minutes from Hubin pier.

places.name: Hong Kong

visits.text: Flight Guilin→Hong Kong. Victoria Peak (Sky Terrace 428 m). Peak Tram (1888), climbs in 6 min. Trail of 3.5 km around the peak.
Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade, Avenue of Stars. A Symphony of Lights every evening at 8pm—over 40 illuminated buildings.
Tian Tan Big Buddha on Lantau (1993, 34 m, 250 tonnes, 268 steps). Po Lin Monastery (1920s), vegetarian lunch of the monks.
Tai O fishing village (30 min by bus). Stilt houses, dried shrimp paste.
Mong Kok in Kowloon: flower, goldfish, bird markets and Ladies' Market. One of the world's most densely populated districts.
Man Mo Temple, Hollywood Road (mid-19th century). Suspended incense coils burning for weeks.
Mid-Levels Escalator (800 m, 135 m elevation, 1993). World's longest outdoor covered escalator.

activities.text: Star Ferry (founded 1880s, company since 1898). Crossing Tsim Sha Tsui-Central in 8-10 min.
Ngong Ping 360 cable car (25 min) from Tung Chung. Crystal Cabin option (glass floor).
Evening at Temple Street Night Market: dim sum, wonton, egg waffle, fortune tellers, street Cantonese opera.

visits.text: Hongcun (宏村), founded in 1131 during the Song Dynasty by the Wang family, is designed in the shape of an ox. The Moon Pond (月沼) at the center, dug in 1403, is the village's most photographed setting: white-walled, gray-roofed Hui houses reflect in the still water. This is where Ang Lee filmed several scenes of Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon (2000). UNESCO-listed the same year.

Hongcun's 150 Ming-Qing residences are the region's finest collection of Hui architecture. Lexu Hall and Jingde Tang are open to visitors: carved bracket roof frames, light-well courtyards (天井), blue stone paving. Explore the side lanes for houses still inhabited.

activities.text: Set yourself up by the Moon Pond before 7am with a sketchbook or camera. Chinese amateur painters are there before you - genuine open-air artistic culture at Hongcun. It's also the hour when residents go about their business before the influx of tour groups.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Lotus Peak (莲花峰) reaches 1,864 m - Huangshan's highest summit. The mountain was named by imperial decree in 747 CE; legend has it that Yellow Emperor Huangdi came here seeking the elixir of immortality 2,600 years BCE. Since the 16th century, it's been the favorite subject of the Shanshui painting school. UNESCO-listed in 1990.

White Goose Ridge (白鹅岭) is directly accessible from the Yungu cable car on the east side. From there, a trail connects to Bright Summit in 2 hours via the ridges. It's along this route that you'll find Huangshan's most famous pines: Welcoming Guest Pine, Couple Pine, Umbrella Pine - each has a name, a silhouette from classical painting.

Bright Summit (光明顶, 1,860 m) is the logistical hub for summit stays: this is where hotels, the weather center, and high-altitude restaurants concentrate. Book the West Sea Hotel several weeks in advance. The flat summit allows a clear 360° view even in light mist. Bring warm clothing even in May - 10°C at night is common.

The West Sea Grand Canyon (西海大峡谷) is Huangshan's most spectacular hike - a 5-hour loop that descends into the gorges then ascends by monorail. The site's most dramatic granite formations concentrate here, with stone columns emerging from the clouds. The West Sea Pavilion (built in 1935) offers the widest view over the entire canyon.
Huangshan Hot Springs (黄山温泉), at the foot of the mountain on the Yungu side, let you decompress after two days of hiking. The water emerges at 42°C and the outdoor pools face the forest.

activities.text: Take the Yungu cable car on the east side then hike to connect Bright Summit, Lotus Peak, and White Goose Ridge - count 5 to 6 hours for this classic circuit. The trails are in excellent condition, with steps carved into the rock on steep passages.
Set yourself up at sunset on Bright Summit or Celestial Capital Peak. The late-day light tints the granite peaks from gold to orange - Huangshan's other magic hour, besides sunrise.

Wake at 5am for sunrise from Bright Summit or Celestial Capital Peak. The sea of clouds below the ridges is the spectacle that defines Huangshan - it's why Ming painters would set up here for months. Best period: November to May, after rain.
Descend the mountain by cable car after sunrise, retrieve your luggage at Tangkou, and drive toward Hongcun (25 km, 40 min). Lunch in Tangkou village before departure.

Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Conquer Mount Hua (Huashan), one of China's five sacred mountains. The western peak rises to 2,154 meters, accessible via cable car or challenging hiking trails including the infamous 'Soldier's Path' with 4,000 steep steps carved into sheer cliffs.

Experience the North Peak via the Gorge Trail or the more demanding routes that 'break the legs' with endless stairs. As you ascend, spectacular panoramas unfold across the surrounding plains and distant peaks.

Discover ancient temples and shrines perched on precipitous ledges throughout the mountain. Taoist monks have practiced here for millennia, drawn by the mountain's spiritual power and breathtaking natural beauty.

activities.text: Challenge yourself on the famous plank walk, narrow wooden boards bolted to vertical cliff faces offering heart-stopping views. This iconic attraction draws adventurers from around the world.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan

visits.text: Jiayuguan Fortress (Jiāyùguān), built in 1372 during the Ming dynasty, marks the western terminus of the Great Wall of China. The fort rises between two mountain chains—the snow-capped Qilian Mountains to the south and the Mazong Mountains to the north—in a natural choke point called "the most impregnable pass under heaven." Its outer walls measure 733 meters in perimeter and 11 meters high, pierced by three gates. According to local legend, the fort's architect calculated the exact number of bricks needed; when construction finished, exactly one brick remained—you can still see it resting on a ledge above the west gate, proof of the calculation's precision.

The Overhanging Great Wall (Xuánbì Chángchéng), 8 km north of the fort, is a section that climbs the cliff face of the Mazong Mountains at a 45° angle. Built in 1539, it's nicknamed "Gansu's little Badaling." From the summit, views stretch across the Gobi Desert to the north and snow-capped mountains to the south. The Great Wall Museum at the fort's base traces the history of the entire 21,196-km defensive network built between the 7th century BC and the 16th century.

activities.text: Walk Jiayuguan Fort's ramparts at sunset. The battlements overlook the Gobi Desert to the west—beyond the fort, Silk Road travelers entered unknown territory. This is where exiles and merchants left Chinese civilization behind, a moment Tang dynasty poets compared to "departing the world."

days_plan.itin_title: China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: Huanglong ("Yellow Dragon") is a 700 km² UNESCO site in the Min Mountains. Its main attraction: 3.6 km of yellow travertine terraces formed by calcium deposits from a hot spring, creating hundreds of tiered pools filled with turquoise, blue, and green water. The Five-Color Pool, at the summit at 3,576 meters, is the trail's high point—its waters shift hue with the angle of light.

Jiuzhaigou Valley ("Valley of Nine Villages") covers 720 km² in the Min Mountains, between 2,000 and 4,764 meters elevation. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1992, it takes its name from nine Tibetan villages. Its 114 lakes in turquoise, blue, green, and violet waters owe their colors to high calcium carbonate concentration and algae. Lake bottoms are visible at depths of several meters.

Five-Flower Lake, in the Rize Valley, is considered the most beautiful: its translucent water reveals a bottom of submerged logs between travertine beaches, in gradients of turquoise and emerald. Further up, Panda Lake owes its name to the giant pandas that once came to drink here—around 20 pandas still live within the park boundaries.
Nuorilang Waterfall, 270 meters wide, is China's widest travertine waterfall. Partially damaged by the magnitude 7 earthquake of August 2017, it was restored and the park reopened in 2019.

Long Lake (Chang Hai), in Zechawa Valley (left branch of the Y), is the largest, deepest (103 meters), and highest lake in Jiuzhaigou, at 3,150 meters. Stretching 7.5 km, surrounded by forested mountains, it has no outflow stream—water seeps through the karst bottom. Local legend speaks of a monster in its depths.

Five-Color Pond (Wucai Chi), despite its modest size, offers Jiuzhaigou's most colorful waters—blue, green, yellow, turquoise, and violet mingle according to depth, minerals, and algae. It's one of the smallest lakes but the most intense in color.

Mirror Lake (Jing Hai), in Shuzheng Valley, perfectly reflects the surrounding mountains and forests in its motionless waters. The Tibetan village of Shuzheng, one of the original nine villages, preserves traditional houses and prayer wheels.

Free morning in the town of Zhangzha, at the exit of Jiuzhaigou Valley. Tibetan shops offer turquoise jewelry, incense, thangkas, and ornate knives. The food market features plateau products: matsutake mushrooms, goji berries, wild honey, and dried yak cheese.

activities.text: Ascend on foot or by cable car to the summit of Huanglong (3,576 m), then descend on foot along the travertine terraces (3.6 km, roughly 3 hours). Oxygen bottles are sold at the entrance for visitors sensitive to altitude. The trail crosses conifer forest, waterfalls, and Taoist temples before reaching the pools.

Shuttle and walking route through Rize Valley (right branch of the Y): the bus climbs to the primeval forest at the valley's end, then the descent is on foot along wooden boardwalks that skirt the lakes. Allow 4 to 5 hours for the complete valley.

Route through Zechawa Valley and return via Shuzheng Valley (main trunk of the Y): Pearl Shoal's cascading waterfalls, Shuzheng Tibetan village, Rhinoceros Lake. Wooden boardwalks cross conifer forests and bamboo groves—habitat of the giant panda and Sichuan golden monkey.

Transfer to Jiuzhai-Huanglong Airport (1h30 drive, altitude 3,448 m) for a flight to Chengdu (45 min), Xi'an, or Beijing. The airport, perched on a mountain, offers a final panorama over the snow-capped peaks of the Min Mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
