Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: Kaifeng served as the capital of the Northern Song dynasty (960–1127), a city of one million inhabitants—the largest in the world at the time. The Iron Pagoda of Youguo Temple, built in 1049 under Emperor Renzong, stands as its symbol. Octagonal, thirteen stories, 56.88 meters: it's not made of iron but glazed brown bricks that mimic rust from a distance. Over 1,600 sculptures of Buddhas, dancers, and flowers adorn its walls. It has survived 38 earthquakes and 6 Yellow River floods.

Millennium City Park (Qingming Riverside Landscape Garden) recreates life in 12th-century Kaifeng on a grand scale, based on the famous scroll painting "Along the River During the Qingming Festival" by Zhang Zeduan. It's highly theatrical: actors in Song costumes, horse races, cockfights, merchant streets, puppetry. For kids and enthusiasts of material culture alike, it's an excellent gateway.

Daxiangguo Temple, right in downtown Kaifeng, was founded in 555 and played a pivotal role in the development of Chinese Buddhism under the Song. Its Hall of a Thousand Arms houses a statue of Guanyin carved from a single ginkgo trunk, dated to the 18th century—truly spectacular.

Kaifeng's former Jewish quarter is one of the most surprising chapters in Chinese history. A Jewish community settled here around the 10th–11th century, likely arriving from Persia via the Silk Road. For 800 years, they kept the Torah, spoke Hebrew, and built a synagogue (destroyed in the 19th century). The current memorial, modest, displays a few steles and an exhibition. You need a local guide to truly understand—the site itself is poorly curated.

Longting Park (Dragon Pavilion), north of the city, occupies the site of the former Song imperial palace. The current pavilion dates to the Qing, but it evokes the vanished grandeur. In October–November, it's the heart of Kaifeng's Chrysanthemum Festival (held since the Song dynasty), with over 1,000 varieties on display.

Kaifeng Fu recreates the tribunal of Judge Bao Zheng, an upright 11th-century magistrate who became a folk hero (Bao the Righteous). The visit is highly theatrical, but it's a chance to discover the Song judicial system: prison, interrogation room, instruments. There's a trial reenactment every two hours—in Chinese, but comedic and expressive.

activities.text: In the evening, dive into the Drum Tower night market (Gulou). Kaifeng ranks among China's great culinary cities: try the Kaifeng-style xiaolongbao (juicy steamed dumplings), hu la tang (spicy morning soup), and sesame flatbreads with stuffing. It's lively until 11 pm, with a warm Chinese family atmosphere.

Rent a bike and circle the Kaifeng city walls (12 km), rebuilt under the Qing. The remarkable part: beneath your feet lie five successive layers of buried cities, including Song-era Bianliang, buried under Yellow River silt. At dusk, a 3D show is projected onto the walls.

For authentic street food, venture off the tourist path to Madao Street (马道街) or Shudian Street (书店街, Bookshop Street). Local Kaifeng residents eat xiaolongbao from Diyilou (the city's historic brand, founded in 1922) and drink iced chrysanthemum tea in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

visits.text: Upper Langde Miao Village, 30 km from Kaili, is a hamlet of wooden stilted houses clinging to the mountainside. Visitors are greeted with a welcome ceremony: women in embroidered costumes offer rice wine in water buffalo horns. The village preserves songs, lusheng (bamboo mouth organ) dances, and traditional embroidery without compromising to mass tourism.

Matang Gejia Village, 19 km from Kaili, houses 400 members of an officially unrecognized Miao subgroup—they consider themselves China's "57th ethnic group." According to their legend, they descend from Hou Yi, the mythical archer who shot down nine suns. The women practice beeswax batik with unique geometric patterns.

The Qiandongnan Nationalities Museum, in central Kaili, presents costumes, silver jewelry, musical instruments, and agricultural tools of the Miao and Dong peoples across eleven halls. The silver ornament collection—crowns, breastplates, necklaces—illustrates the metal's importance in Miao culture: a complete bridal set can weigh over 10 kg.

activities.text: Batik workshop in Matang: artisans teach how to draw patterns on fabric using a stylus dipped in hot beeswax, before indigo dyeing. The complete process (drawing, soaking, rinsing, drying) takes about 2 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: En route to Kangding, stop at Luding to see the Luding Chain Bridge (泸定桥), built in 1706 by order of Emperor Kangxi. Its 13 iron chains stretched across the Dadu River gorges make it one of China's oldest suspension bridges. In 1935, Mao's troops crossed it under enemy fire during the Long March—an episode that founded the Red Army legend. Allow 30 minutes.

Kangding (康定) marks the frontier between lowland China and the Tibetan world of Kham. At 2,560m, it's your first night at altitude—your body begins to adapt. The town is wedged in a deep gorge crossed by two rivers. Lhamtso Ngachu Monastery, in the heights, dates to the 17th century and belongs to the Nyingma school. Climb up in late afternoon: the light on the mountains is exceptional.

Kangding's old town blends Tibetan and Han architecture in commercial alleyways where monks buy yak butter alongside tourists from Chengdu. Paoma Square (跑马山), at the foot of the mountain of the same name, is the town's heart—locals gather there evenings to dance in circles, a living Khampa tradition.

activities.text: Paoma Mountain (跑马山, 3,300m) is reached on foot or by cable car from downtown. Panoramic view over the city and, on clear days, of Gongga (7,556m)—Sichuan's highest peak. This is where your physical relationship with altitude truly begins. Walk slowly, drink plenty of water.
Dinner at a Tibetan restaurant in Kangding: order momo (yak meat steamed dumplings) and salted yak butter tea. It's rich, it's hot, it's plateau fuel. Might as well get used to it tonight.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Journey along the Karakoram Highway, one of the world's most spectacular mountain roads connecting China to Pakistan. The route climbs through dramatic gorges and high plateaus with views of 7,000-meter peaks.
Arrive at Lake Karakul, an alpine jewel at 3,600 meters reflecting the snow-capped peak of Muztagh Ata (7,546m) in its crystal waters. Kyrgyz herders graze yaks on the shores of this sacred lake.
Experience the high-altitude landscape where the Pamir, Karakoram, and Kunlun ranges converge. This 'roof of the world' offers some of Earth's most dramatic mountain scenery.

activities.text: Stay in a traditional Kyrgyz yurt by the lakeshore, experiencing nomadic hospitality with fresh yogurt, butter tea, and stories of life in these remote highlands.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan

visits.text: Id Kah Mosque (Àitígǎěr Qīngzhēn Sì), in Kashgar's heart, is China's largest mosque. Founded in 1442, it can accommodate up to 20,000 worshippers in its courtyard and prayer hall. Its yellow facade flanked by two minarets faces the city's central square, the convergence point of social and commercial life. Friday prayer gathers thousands from across the region.

Kashgar's Old Town (Kāshí Gǔchéng), one of Central Asia's most ancient quarters, is a labyrinth of pisé alleyways, houses with carved wooden balconies, and interior courtyards. Artisans work copper, wood, and pottery in workshops opening onto the street. The potters' quarter, backed against a clay cliff to the northeast, has produced glazed jars and plates in Kashgar's distinctive style for centuries. The old town was partially restored but retains the topography and atmosphere of a Silk Road oasis city.

The Apak Hoja Mausoleum (Xiāngfēi Mù), 5 km northeast of downtown, is Xinjiang's most important Islamic funerary monument. Built in 1640, it houses the tombs of five generations of the Hoja family in a hall topped by a dome tiled in blue and white faience. Chinese legend associates it with the "Fragrant Concubine," a Uyghur princess who supposedly naturally exhaled a musky scent.

Kashgar's Sunday Grand Bazaar (Yèkèshànbā Bāzhā), direct heir to Silk Road markets, is one of Central Asia's largest open-air markets. Thousands of merchants from surrounding countryside and neighboring countries (Pakistan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan) sell carpets, silks, spices, knives, leather, and dried fruits. The livestock market on the outskirts sees sheep, goats, donkeys, horses, and yaks change hands—deals sealed with a handshake and tea.

activities.text: Watch local artisans at work in old town workshops—coppersmiths, knifemakers, and musical instrument makers maintaining skills passed through generations of Uyghur craftspeople.
Meet Uyghur families in surrounding villages.
Wander through commercial districts and observe the Han and Uyghur cultural blend.

Walk through old town artisan workshops: forging Uyghur knives (pìchàk) with bone or horn handles, pottery wheels in the potters' quarter, stringed instrument making (dutar, rawap). Artisans work at street level, allowing you to watch their hands at work. Late afternoon, sample polo (Uyghur lamb pilaf with carrots) and tea in a traditional teahouse.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: The village of Langmusi ("Tiger Temple" in Tibetan—a tiger cave once stood at the monastery site) is split in two by a river marking the border between Gansu and Sichuan. Each province has its own monastery, school, police station, and hospital. Tibetan Buddhists and Hui Muslims coexist in this high-altitude hamlet (3,300 m) with an alpine-village feel.

Kirti Monastery, on the Sichuan side, is the larger of the two: founded in 1413, expanded in 1748, it houses around 700 monks. Its particularity is preserving the intact body of the 5th Kirti Lama, displayed every 3 to 5 days. Behind the monastery, Namo Gorge marks the source of the Bailong (White Dragon) River.

Sertri Monastery, on the Gansu side, dates from 1748. Smaller but recognizable by its golden roofs, it overlooks the town from a hill. Above, a sky burial (jhator) site is still in use—bodies of the deceased are offered to vultures according to Tibetan Buddhist tradition. The place is sacred and visitors must not attend ceremonies.

Namo Gorge, at the back of Kirti Valley, shelters the source of the Bailong River in a cave lined with stalactites. The trail follows the river between moss-covered limestone walls in a primeval forest atmosphere.

activities.text: Hike through the red gorges behind Sertri Monastery. The trail climbs through wildflower meadows toward Red Mountain (Chongpoma), a red sandstone formation visible from the entire village. At the summit, prayer flags flutter facing snowy peaks. Allow 2 hours round trip.

Horseback riding through the grasslands around Langmusi. Local agencies offer outings from half a day to three days through alpine pastures, nomadic camps, and remote valleys. Tibetan horses, small and sure-footed, are accustomed to steep trails.
Meal at a Hui (Muslim) restaurant in Langmusi: hand-pulled noodles, grilled lamb skewers, flatbread. The Tibetan-Muslim coexistence gives the village a dual cuisine found nowhere else.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: The Gansu Provincial Museum, on the Yellow River's south bank, holds over 350,000 artifacts including the "Galloping Horse" (Mǎ Tà Fēiyàn), a bronze statuette from the Eastern Han dynasty (25-220 AD) depicting a horse at full gallop balanced on a flying swallow—now the emblem of Chinese tourism. The Silk Road galleries display silks, Roman coins, and Buddhist objects found along the Hexi Corridor.

The Zhongshan Bridge (Zhōngshān Qiáo), the first permanent bridge built across the Yellow River, was erected in 1907 with materials imported from Germany and assembled by American engineers. Before its construction, people crossed the Yellow River on rafts made from inflated sheepskins (yángpí fázi), a method used for centuries. From the bridge, climb to White Pagoda Park (Báitǎ Shān Gōngyuán), built during the Yuan dynasty (13th century) to honor a Tibetan lama who died in Lanzhou en route to Genghis Khan's court.

activities.text: Sample Lanzhou beef noodle soup (Lánzhōu niúròu miàn), considered China's most widespread street food—an estimated 100,000+ restaurants nationwide serve it. The clear spiced broth, hand-pulled noodles made before your eyes, sliced beef, cilantro, and chili oil form the traditional recipe's "five colors."

days_plan.itin_title: China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: The Chengdu-Lhasa flight takes 2 hours 15 minutes and lands at Gonggar Airport, 60km from the city. Don't visit anything on arrival day. Lhasa is at 3,650m—after Litang and Yading, your body is better prepared than a direct traveler, but flight fatigue and pressure change demand rest. After transfer to the hotel, a short walk in the Barkhor neighborhood does no harm.

Barkhor Street (八廓街) is the circumambulation route around Jokhang Temple—pilgrims have turned clockwise for centuries. Vendors offer prayer beads, thangkas, and yak butter under canvas awnings. It's lively, dense, real. The first evening in Lhasa is spent here, simply, hands in pockets.

The Potala Palace (布达拉宫) rises on the Red Hill at 3,700m, 13 stories for 110 meters height, 1,000 rooms. Begun in 1645 by the 5th Dalai Lama, Lobsang Gyatso, it took 43 years to complete—and this Dalai Lama died before the end, his death concealed for 12 years to avoid interrupting construction. It was the seat of Tibetan government until the 14th Dalai Lama's flight to India in 1959. The Red Palace houses the golden funerary stupas of eight Dalai Lamas. Tickets must be booked online with real registration—no tickets on-site.

Jokhang Temple (大昭寺) is Tibet's spiritual heart. Founded in 639 by King Songtsen Gampo to house the Jowo statue—a golden Buddha brought by his Nepalese wife Bhrikuti—it rises on 4 levels. Its historic core, dendrochronologically dated to the 7th century, is the only surviving ancient Indian wooden architecture in the entire world. It houses 800 metal sculptures and thousands of thangkas. Photography prohibited inside.

Drepung Monastery (哲蚌寺) was, before 1959, the world's largest monastery with 10,000 monks. Founded in 1416, it perches 5km west of Lhasa on a white hill. Only a few hundred monks remain today, but the main assembly hall, thangka chapels, and interior alleyways are intact. In the morning, monks debate in the courtyards.

Sera Monastery (色拉寺) is famous for its monk debates, held each afternoon (except Sunday) in the inner courtyards between 3-5 PM. Monks clash by clapping hands, pointing fingers, laughing—it's a Buddhist dialectic learning method codified for centuries. Sera was founded in 1419 and housed 5,000 monks before 1959.

Norbulingka (罗布林卡), the Dalai Lama's former summer palace, is a 36-hectare park west of the city. Built in the 18th century by the 7th Dalai Lama, it's been a UNESCO World Heritage extension of the Potala since 2001. It's less visited than Jokhang—local Tibetans come to picnic with family under the willows. A haven of calm.

Chakpori Hill (药王山), facing the Potala from across the main avenue, is the best viewpoint of the Potala Palace—no queue, no ticket, no crowd. Climb on foot in 20 minutes. Dusk over the Potala from this hill is the photo everyone seeks.

Return to Lhasa in late afternoon. Last evening walk at Barkhor—you see it differently after experiencing Rongbuk. The pilgrims circling Jokhang with their rosaries sometimes come from regions even more remote than where you went. Sit on a stupa's steps and observe.

activities.text: Rest—that's Day 10's main activity in Lhasa. Hydrate, sleep early, don't push. Tourists who ignore this simple rule find themselves bedridden on Day 11 with severe altitude sickness. Your body needs 12-24 hours to recalibrate its hemoglobin production.

Walk the Barkhor kora at sunrise (from 6 AM): pilgrims circle Jokhang with their prayer wheels, rosaries, full prostrations on the flagstones. The morning's grazing sunlight on the golden roofs, incense smoke, and mantra hum compose a scene that midday tourists won't know.

Dinner at a Tibetan restaurant in the Barkhor quarter: thukpa (yak noodle soup), yak meat momo, chang (fermented barley beer). Some good addresses in the alleyways north of Jokhang, recognizable by red banners and kitchen sounds.

Explore the old Tibetan town on foot or by rental bike: the Shöl and Ramoche neighborhoods, north of Jokhang, still have white-facade houses painted with typical black and red motifs. It's what remains of pre-1950 Lhasa, amid modern buildings.

Travel shopping in shops around Barkhor: sutra scrolls, copper prayer wheels, carved mani stones, singing bowls. Always negotiate—displayed prices are for tourists. Shops run by Tibetans are generally more authentic than big Chinese stores.
Last meal in Lhasa and luggage check. Gonggar Airport is 60km from the center—allow minimum 1.5 hours. Some flights depart at dawn.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Litang at 4,014m altitude—higher than the summit of Mont Blanc. The town is nicknamed "the holy city on the snowy land." Chöde Gompa Monastery (长青春科尔寺) dominates the north of town from a hill. Founded in 1580 by the 3rd Dalai Lama, Sonam Gyatso, during a passage through the region, it's Kham's largest Gelugpa monastery. At its peak, it housed 5,000 monks and oversaw 113 satellite monasteries.

The birthplace of the 7th Dalai Lama (Kelzang Gyatso, 1708-1757) stands in the town center—an ordinary Tibetan dwelling with a stupa in the courtyard, surrounded by devotees circling continuously. The 10th Dalai Lama, Tsultrim Gyatso (1816), was also born in Litang. This double birth made the town a special place of devotion for Tibetan Buddhists.

Litang's mani stone wall is one of Kham's longest. Thousands of stones carved with the sacred syllable Om Mani Padme Hum stretch for several hundred meters. The giant prayer wheel adjoining it is reputed to be the world's largest. Women and men in traditional costume circle it endlessly.

Second morning in Litang: return to Chöde Gompa Monastery during morning services, around 7 AM. Monks chant in the great prayer hall—the sound of thighbone trumpets (kangling) and drums rises to the terraced roofs. The art collection is remarkable: saddle of the 3rd Dalai Lama, Ming Dynasty frescoes, ancient manuscripts. No explanatory panels—just the works and the silence.

The Litang plateau extends for dozens of kilometers around the town. Ask your driver to take you 10-15km from the center: the nomads' black tents, herds of black yaks in the blonde grasses, and absolute silence form a tableau you won't soon forget.

activities.text: Wander through the lanes of Litang market in the morning. Khampa nomads from surrounding villages—women with braids adorned with coral and turquoise, men in burgundy chuba with their large knives at their belts—come to trade goods here. Don't pull out your camera immediately: observe first, earn their gaze, then ask.

Walk slowly through town—at 4,014m, even climbing 50 steps leaves you breathless. This is your first real night at very high altitude: rest this afternoon, hydrate abundantly, don't push yourself. Your body is producing extra red blood cells—give it time.

If you're here in early August, the Litang Horse Festival (赛马节) is among the Tibetan plateau's largest: Khampa horsemen gallop at full speed picking up silk scarves from the ground. Outside this date, the festival atmosphere isn't there—but the town's soul remains palpable.

Prepare for departure to Daocheng: check the G227 road condition with your driver. After June rains, some sections are cut off by landslides. The Litang-Daocheng journey takes 3-4 hours under best conditions.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: The Ping'an rice terraces in the Longji massif (Dragon's Backbone), about 80 km from Guilin, spread thousands of stepped plots between 600 and 800 meters altitude. Construction of these terraces began under the Yuan dynasty (1271–1368) and concluded in the early Qing dynasty (1644–1911)—over 400 years of work by Zhuang communities. The "Nine Dragons and Five Tigers" viewpoint shows nine ridges undulating like dragons bending to drink from the Jinsha River, while five tiger-shaped rocks frame them. The second panorama, "Seven Stars Surrounding the Moon," shows seven small hills encircling a crescent-shaped rice paddy.

Ping'an Zhuang village was the first Longji terraces site opened to visitors. Its traditional wooden stilt houses, the oldest dating from the Qing dynasty, cling to the slope between terraces. The Zhuang, one of China's 55 ethnic minority groups, perpetuate their architecture, textile crafts, and mountain cuisine—taste rice cooked in bamboo tubes (zhútǒng fàn), fragrant and smoky.

The Jinkeng Dazhai terraces, Longji's most extensive, cover 40 to 50 km² between 300 and 1,100 meters altitude. Dazhai village is inhabited by Red Yao people, recognizable by their spectacular hairstyles—women cut their hair only once in a lifetime and coil it into buns that can exceed a meter long. Three viewpoints mark the ascent: viewpoint #2, "Thousand Layers to Heaven" (Qiāncéng Tiāntī), closest to the village, shows terraces stacked like a giant stairway; #1, "Music of West Mountain" (Xīshān Sháolè), at 1,180 meters, is the highest and offers the best sunrise view; #3, "Golden Buddha Peak" (Jīnfó Dǐng), is accessible by cable car from Dazhai's parking area.

Huangluo Yao village, below the Dazhai terraces, is listed in the Guinness Book of Records for the highest concentration of long-haired women. The Red Yao perpetuate the tradition of the "Clothes Drying Festival" (Shài Yījiē), an annual celebration where brightly embroidered costumes are displayed on wooden balconies. You'll discover embroidery techniques, indigo dyeing, and local hot pepper sauce making.

activities.text: Hike from Ping'an to the old Zhuang village of Longji (Gǔzhuāng) via a flagstone trail descending through the terraces. Allow 40 minutes to 1h30 depending on pace. The ancient village preserves the region's best-kept stilt houses, some over 150 years old. The return can be made on the same path or by shuttle.

Hike from Dazhai to Ping'an crossing through Zhongliu village—a 4 to 5 hour route through the most remote and least-visited terraces. The trail alternates between ridges and valleys, with changing views over the rice fields at each pass. Bring good shoes—the path is narrow and slippery in wet weather.

days_plan.itin_title: Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces
Karst Heartlands: Guilin, Yangshuo & Longji Rice Terraces

places.name: Longsheng

visits.text: Drive from Fenghuang to Longsheng (~5h). Longji rice terraces ('Dragon's Backbone'), 100 km north of Guilin. Sculpted by the Zhuang and Yao starting from the Yuan dynasty (13th century), 650 years of labor. 66 km², altitudes from 300 to 1,100 m. In spring the flooded terraces reflect the sky; in autumn, they turn golden.
Zhuang village of Ping'an: stilt houses, 'Nine Dragons and Five Tigers' and 'Seven Stars Around the Moon' viewpoints.

activities.text: Hike between Ping'an viewpoints (~1h30) through terraces and villages. At Huangluo village, encounter with Yao women whose hair exceeds 1.80 m in length.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

map_keywords: Nine Dragons Five Tigers Viewpoint Longsheng

visits.text: The Xi'an–Luoyang train takes just 1.5 hours and drops you at Luoyang Longmen, ten minutes from the grottoes. Head straight there in the morning: 2,345 niches carved into the limestone cliffs along the Yi River over a 1 km stretch, with nearly 110,000 Buddhist statues sculpted between 493 and the mid-8th century, inscribed as a UNESCO site in 2000. This is China's most breathtaking stone sculpture ensemble, rivaling Mogao and Yungang.

The Fengxian Temple, on the west bank, is the crown jewel of the site. The central statue of Vairocana Buddha stands 17.14 meters tall, carved between 672 and 675 during the reign of Empress Wu Zetian, who is said to have lent her own features to the face. The heads of secondary statues were decapitated in the 1920s–1930s by Western collectors or during the Cultural Revolution. Many now reside at the Met in New York or the Atkinson Museum in Kansas City.

Cross the bridge to the east bank, far less crowded. The Xiangshan Temple, rebuilt in 1707 under the Qing, and the garden of Bai Juyi (a Tang poet who spent his final days in Luoyang) offer panoramic views of the western cliffs. If you can, stay for the evening visit: the site reopens from 7 pm to 11 pm April–October, separate ticket at 90 yuan, far fewer people, and the lighting brings the Buddha to life.

According to tradition, the White Horse Temple is China's first Buddhist temple, founded in 68 AD under Emperor Ming of the Eastern Han, 12 km east of Luoyang. The emperor is said to have dreamed of a golden being and sent emissaries to India; they returned with two monks—Kasyapa Matanga and Dharmaratna—and sutras carried on a white horse, hence the name. The current buildings date mainly from the Ming and Qing, but the aura remains intact.

Southeast of the temple, Qiyun Pagoda is one of China's oldest brick pagodas. Rebuilt in 1175 during the Jin dynasty, it rises 35 meters. Clap your hands at the south side of the pagoda: the echo sounds uncannily like croaking frogs—an unexplained acoustic phenomenon that has delighted visitors for centuries.

The Luoyang Museum, opened in its new building in 2011, showcases one of the country's finest collections of Shang and Zhou bronzes, along with Tang tricolor (sancai) ceramics emblematic of the city. Plan on two unhurried hours—it's free with your passport.

Guanlin Temple, south of the city, houses the presumed tomb of General Guan Yu's head. A hero of the Three Kingdoms era (3rd century), he was deified as Guandi. This is one of the three major Guan Yu worship sites in China, full of soldiers and police officers coming to pray before his statue.

activities.text: In the evening, stroll through Luoyang's old town (Laocheng), around Lijing Men Street. It's less touristy than Xi'an, more authentic, and you can sample the famous Luoyang Shuixi (the twenty-four water-based dishes), an imperial cuisine descended from the Tang court. The illuminated Lijing Men gate is stunning after dark.

If you're here between April 5 and 25, don't miss the Peony Festival—the flower has been Luoyang's emblem since the Tang dynasty. Wangcheng Park (National Peony Garden) showcases over 1,200 varieties. It's become a national event, so plan extra time for the crowds.

Try a full Luoyang Shuixi meal at a traditional restaurant like Zhen Bu Tong on Zhongzhou Road. Twenty-four successive water- or broth-based dishes derived from Tang imperial cuisine: it's copious, fascinating, and best enjoyed with a group. Budget 150–300 yuan per person.

days_plan.itin_title: Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization
Spiritual Henan & Shaanxi – Tracing the Cradle of Chinese Civilization

places.name: Nanjing

visits.text: The Nanjing Massacre Memorial (侵华日军南京大屠杀遇难同胞纪念馆) is a difficult but essential stop for understanding contemporary China. On December 13, 1937, the Japanese army took the city; for six weeks, official Chinese estimates count 300,000 dead. Built in 1985 on the site of the Jiangdongmen mass grave, the memorial displays 208 bones exhumed in situ and over 1,000 objects. Six million visitors per year.

Ming Xiaoling Tomb (明孝陵), UNESCO-listed in 2003, is the burial site of Zhu Yuanzhang, founder of the Ming Dynasty (reign 1368-1398). The Spirit Way is one of China's most impressive: 800 meters lined with 24 monumental stone statues - elephants, lions, camels, tigers. These guardians have been watching for over 600 years.

Sun Yat-sen Mausoleum (中山陵) imposes with its scale: 392 steps from the entrance arch to the tomb at the summit of Purple Mountain. Built between 1926 and 1929, it mixes Beaux-Arts architecture and nationalist symbolism. Sun Yat-sen (1866-1925), founder of the Republic of China, rests here.

Nanjing's Ming city walls are the world's best-preserved: 35.3 km on the original layout, with 25 km still standing. Built between 1366 and 1393, their bricks bear the kiln's name and the foreman's - a unique quality control measure. The section around Zhonghua Gate is the most accessible for walking the ramparts.

The Confucius Temple area (夫子庙, Fuzi Miao) runs along the Qinhuai River - the former imperial examination district where thousands of scholar candidates came to try their luck for centuries. The evening atmosphere with red lanterns and boat rides on the Qinhuai is genuinely pleasant.

Xuanwu Lake (玄武湖), at the foot of the northern Ming walls, is a 4.73 km² park in the heart of the city. Its five islands connected by causeways served as training grounds for the Ming imperial fleet. Pleasant walk away from tourist bustle.

activities.text: Stroll through Zhongshan Park (紫金山) between the mausoleum and Ming tomb - forest of plane trees and cypresses, peaceful trails, artificial lake. One of China's finest urban walks, 5 km from downtown.

Dine in the Xinjiekou district. Nanjing specialties: ya xue fen si (vermicelli with duck blood), pan mian (crab stir-fried noodles), salted duck - Nanjing's salted duck has been a national institution since the Ming Dynasty.
Climb onto the Ming wall ramparts in the evening from Zhonghua Gate - the night view over the city and illuminated defenses is striking. The section toward the Qinhuai River takes 45 minutes on foot.

Take an evening boat on the Qinhuai River - the Confucius Temple quarter viewed from the water, with its lanterns and reflected pavilions, is Nanjing's nocturnal postcard. Departs from Fuzi Miao pier.

visits.text: The Tianluokeng cluster forms the "four dishes and one soup": four round or oval tulou surrounding a square tulou, seen from above. It was by spotting these circular shapes on satellite images in the 1960s that the CIA believed it had discovered nuclear missile silos—an anecdote systematically cited in guidebooks, probably exaggerated but now inseparable from the place.

Taxia village stretches along a stream bordered by stone bridges and 24 ten-meter-high honor poles erected to celebrate clan members who obtained academic titles. The village counts 47 tulou of varied shapes among the mountains.

Yuchanglou, in Xiaban village, is a 14th-century round tulou whose interior pillars lean up to 15 degrees without the structure having collapsed in over 700 years. This "leaning tower" of Fujian intrigues architects.

activities.text: Bike ride between tulou in Nanjing valley. Country roads cross tea plantations, groves of 13 centuries-old banyans (over 300 years old) along the river, and inhabited Hakka hamlets.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: High-speed train Datong→Pingyao (~3h). Fortified city, UNESCO since 1997, China's last intact walled city. Ramparts erected in rammed earth as early as 827 BC, then reinforced in stone in 1370 under the Ming. Six kilometers in perimeter, 12 m high, 6 gates, 72 bastions. Turtle silhouette seen from the sky.
The Rishengchang Bank (1823), China's first bank. Invention of interbank transfers. Martial arts-trained escorts for money transport.
The old county yamen (26,000 m², rebuilt in 1346). Daily reenactments of ancient trials.
Shuanglin Temple, 7 km away, 1,500 years old. Over 1,000 colored clay Buddhist statues, including a 26-armed Guanyin. UNESCO with Pingyao.
The Confucius Temple, the oldest preserved in China. Dacheng Hall from 1163 (Jin dynasty).

activities.text: Strolling the lanes parallel to Ming Qing Jie. Sample mao'erduo (Shanxi 'cat's ear' noodles).
Sunset from the ramparts—north section, view over the rooftops and Shanxi mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie
Grand China Journey: Beijing to Hong Kong via Zhangjiajie

visits.text: Qinghai Lake (Kokonor in Mongolian, meaning "Blue Sea") is China's largest lake: 4,500 km² of saltwater at roughly 3,200 meters elevation, framed by four mountain ranges peaking between 3,600 and 5,200 meters. Elliptical in shape, it freezes solid in winter. Tibetan Buddhist pilgrims circumambulate the lake—360 km that takes 18 days on horseback or 23 days on foot.

The Erlangjian scenic area on the south shore offers the best lake access. A jetty extends into the turquoise water with views of the snow-capped Datong Mountains to the north. On clear days, you can make out the sand dunes of Shadao Island on the far shore. In July, fields of flowering rapeseed encircle the lake in a brilliant yellow belt.

Bird Island (Niaodao), on the lake's northwest shore, welcomes over 100,000 migratory birds each spring from Southeast Asia—bar-headed geese, whooper swans, cormorants, black-necked cranes. The island itself has been closed to visitors since 2017 to protect habitat, but shoreline observatories allow viewing of the colonies from May through July.
Sand Island (Shadao), on the northeast shore, juxtaposes sand dunes and lake in a striking contrast. The area spans 120 km² with dunes rising several dozen meters high against the turquoise water.

activities.text: Bike tour along Qinghai Lake's south shore. The Tour of Qinghai Lake, a professional cycling race held each July alongside the Tour de France, popularized this route. At 3,200 meters, the effort is real, but the scenery—turquoise lake, grasslands, yaks, snowy peaks—more than compensates.
Visit a Tibetan nomadic camp on the grasslands bordering the lake. Families offer yak butter tea, tsampa (roasted barley flour), and share daily pastoral life—milking yaks, making dried cheese.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

places.name: Riwa Yading

visits.text: Yading Nature Reserve was sanctified in the 8th century by Padmasambhava and described in 1928 in National Geographic by American explorer Joseph Rock—his 60-page report revealed these landscapes to the Western world. Two years later, writer James Hilton published Lost Horizon and invented Shangri-La inspired by these descriptions. From the entrance at Riwa, take the bus to Chonggu Monastery (3,880m)—starting point for all hikes.

Chonggu Monastery sits at the foot of Mount Chenrezig (6,032m, compassion), the highest of the three sacred peaks. Built about 800 years ago, it's damaged and partially restored—but its location, facing Chenrezig's glaciated wall with green pastures in the foreground, compensates everything. It's one of the reserve's most beautiful viewpoints. Allow 45 minutes.

Pearl Lake (珍珠海, 4,100m) is 3km from Chonggu Monastery, entirely on wooden boardwalks. In clear weather, it reflects Chenrezig with surreal clarity—the water is turquoise, the shores white as snow. It's the reserve's most accessible sacred lake. Allow 40 minutes walking from the monastery.

Milk Lake (牛奶海, 4,500m) is Yading's second sacred lake—the climb from Luorong Pastures takes 1.5 hours. Its milky white color comes from the reflection of snow and surrounding limestone rocks. Its shape, seen from above, resembles a teardrop. In fine weather, Jambeyang's glaciers dominate it directly.

Five Color Lake (五色海, 4,600m) sits 100m elevation above Milk Lake. It's the reserve's largest lake (0.7 hectares) and highest. Its color changes with the light—from deep green to sky blue depending on time of day. Arrive in early afternoon for the best effect. Aquatic plants on the bottom color the water from the surface.

activities.text: Hike to Luorong Pastures (洛绒牛场, 4,150m): it's the only place in the reserve where you see all three sacred mountains—Chenrezig (6,032m), Jambeyang (5,958m, wisdom), and Chanadorje (5,958m, power)—simultaneously. Yak herds graze in the sparse grass while snow-capped summits reflect in the Gongga River. Allow 2.5 hours round trip from Chonggu.

Observe Tibetan pilgrims doing kora (circumambulation) around the three mountains. Some come from distant provinces, on foot for weeks. The belief holds that one complete kora equals reciting one million mantras. Walk with them, even for a short section.

Complete hike of the Milk Lake - Five Color Lake circuit from Luorong: this is Yading's "long" route—590m positive elevation gain, about 4-6 hours round trip. Start early (7:30 AM) to avoid groups and enjoy morning light on the glaciers. Walk slowly, drink regularly.
Last sunset over Yading from the heights of Riwa village, facing Chenrezig. The mountain's glaciated face turns orange, then pink, then violet in 20 minutes. This is why you're here.

visits.text: Rongbuk Monastery (绒布寺) stands at 5,019m altitude—the world's highest monastery according to most sources. Founded in 1902 by Nyingma lama Ngawang Tenzin Norbu, it served as starting point and shelter for all major British Everest expeditions in the 1920s. Destroyed during the Cultural Revolution in 1974, it was rebuilt. About twenty monks and nuns still live there. Behind the entrance's white stupa, Everest's north face rises directly—no foreground, no haze, just 8,848 meters of rock and ice.

Rongbuk village and its immediate surroundings are worth exploring on foot from the monastery: meditation caves in the cliff, carved mani walls, prayer flags snapping in the Himalayan's icy wind. Footprints of the founder are indicated in a rock cave above the complex—legend or reality, the place is impressive.

activities.text: Since the north base camp closed to tourists in December 2018 (environmental reasons), Rongbuk is the furthest accessible point. Climbers with ascent permits can go to the real base camp, 8km south. For you, the view from the monastery roof suffices—it's probably the most direct view of the world's highest mountain's north face. Stay until dusk if the sky is clear.

Night at Rongbuk guesthouse (5,000m)—basic, unheated with minimal ventilation, but it's the only available accommodation. Bring a -20°C sleeping bag. The night at this altitude, with Everest in the darkness, is an experience unto itself.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Chengyang's Yongji Bridge, known as the "Wind and Rain Bridge," completed in 1912, is China's most famous Dong covered bridge. Measuring 64.4 meters long, 3.4 meters wide, and 10.6 meters high, it rests on five stone piers topped with multi-tiered wooden pavilions. The entire structure was assembled without a single nail—only through interlocking fir beams. The Sanjiang region counts more than 160 drum towers and a hundred covered bridges.

The eight villages of Chengyang (Ma'an, Pingzhai, Yanzhai, Dazhai, Pingpu, Pingtan, Jichang, Guandong) form an ensemble of stilted houses, drum towers, and water mills along the Linxi River. From the bridge, tea-planted hills, rice paddies, and water mills compose a preserved Dong rural landscape.

Excursion to Biasha Miao Village (2.5-hour drive each way from Sanjiang via Congjiang), nicknamed the "gunmen's village." The men still wear the topknot shaved on the sides, a knife at the belt, and a flintlock rifle—the only community in China authorized to carry firearms. Burials take place under a tree planted at each child's birth.

activities.text: Hike between Chengyang villages along the Linxi River and tea hills. The short circuit (Chengyang → Pingtan → Guandong) crosses rice paddies and plantations over approximately 12 km.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: Flight from Xi'an to Shanghai (about 2.5 hours). The Bund (Waitan), an Anglo-Urdu term meaning 'muddy embankment,' stretches for one mile along the west bank of the Huangpu River. Before the British arrived in 1845, there were only muddy shores here. The Opium Wars opened the port to Westerners, and 52 buildings in various styles—Romanesque, Gothic, Neoclassical, Beaux-Arts, Art Deco—gradually lined the avenue. The most imposing is the former HSBC Building (No. 12), completed in 1923, considered at the time the most sumptuous building between the Suez Canal and the Bering Strait. Next door, the Customs House (No. 13), built in 1927, sports a clock inspired by Big Ben. Across the Bund, on the opposite bank of the river, the Pudong skyline bristles with futuristic skyscrapers.

Yu Garden (Yuyuan), built starting in 1559 by Pan Yunduan—a high-ranking Ming dynasty official—to offer his father a peaceful retreat. Construction took nearly 20 years. Across 5 acres, the garden distills the landscape art of Jiangnan: pavilions, rockeries, lotus ponds, and winding corridors, separated by 'dragon walls' whose ridges undulate and end in a dragon's head. The Great Rockery, designed by master gardener Zhang Nanyang, is a 39-foot-high artificial mountain composed of 2,000 tons of yellow Wukang stones—it's the only surviving work by this master. In front of the entrance, the Nine-Turn Bridge leads to the Huxinting Teahouse in the middle of a pond: its zigzags are meant to block evil spirits, who can only travel in straight lines.

The former French Concession of Shanghai retains its plane trees planted in the early 20th century, Art Deco villas, and shikumen—stone-gate townhouses typical of Shanghai, which appeared in the 1860s and once represented over 60% of the city's housing stock. The Tianzifang quarter, a former residential complex of narrow lanes, has been transformed into art galleries, design studios, and cafés.

The Lujiazui district in Pudong, a special economic zone opened in 1990, concentrates the three towers that define Shanghai's silhouette: Jin Mao Tower (1,380 feet, 88 floors, completed 1999), Shanghai World Financial Center (1,614 feet, 101 floors, 2008), and Shanghai Tower (2,073 feet, 128 floors, 2015)—the world's second-tallest building. The latter's elevator reaches the 119th floor in 55 seconds. From the top, on a clear day, the view extends across the entire megalopolis of 26 million inhabitants.

Nanjing Road (Nanjing Lu), a 3.4-mile shopping artery, links People's Square to the Bund. The pedestrianized western section, opened in 1999, concentrates department stores and brand-name shops along a half-mile stretch. At the street's end, People's Square houses the Shanghai Museum, a building shaped like an ancient bronze vessel displaying collections of Shang and Zhou bronzes, ceramics, and calligraphy.

Excursion to the water town of Zhujiajiao, 29 miles west of Shanghai (about 1 hour on metro line 17). Nicknamed 'Shanghai's Venice,' this 1,700-year-old town preserves lanes and buildings from the Ming and Qing dynasties. Thirty-six stone bridges span the canals, including Fangsheng Bridge—the largest five-arch stone arch bridge in the Shanghai region, built in 1571 by monk Xingchao of Cimen Temple in memory of his mother. Rising 19 feet, it's adorned with a relief of eight dragons surrounding a pearl on the central arch and stone lions at the ends. North Street (Bei Dajie), stretching half a mile, is lined with old buildings and craft shops.

Kezhi Garden, Zhujiajiao's largest private garden, blends traditional Chinese styles with Western influences in an ensemble of pavilions, ponds, and greenery. Its five-story building topped with a pavilion is the old town's highest point.

activities.text: Twilight stroll along the Bund: when lights come on along both riverbanks, the contrast between the colonial architecture and the illuminated Pudong towers is striking.
Wander through the Yuyuan Bazaar surrounding the garden, among souvenir shops, teahouses, and xiaolongbao restaurants (soup dumplings), a Shanghai specialty.
Ascend to the Shanghai Tower observatory (119th floor) for a 360° panorama of the city, then walk down through the Lujiazui financial district.
Traditional boat ride on the canals: boatmen in local costume glide wooden craft under stone bridges and along white houses with black-tiled roofs.
Sample local specialties: zongzi (sticky rice dumplings wrapped in bamboo leaves), braised pork trotters, and sesame cakes from the North Street stalls.

visits.text: Shigatse is Tibet's second city and seat of the Panchen Lama. Tashilhunpo Monastery (扎什伦布寺) was founded in 1447 by the 1st Dalai Lama, Gendun Drup. Its red and white walls extend over 15 hectares at the foot of Tara Hill. The 10th Panchen Lama resided here until his death in 1989. The main hall houses silk thangkas from the Ming period and the 1st Panchen Lama's ceremonial saddle.

Jamkhang Chenmo Chapel houses the 26-meter golden Maitreya, built in 1914 and covered with 279kg of gold. Pilgrims circle its base three times in silence—the head alone measures 4.2 meters. The building's low ceiling makes any perspective impossible: just this mass of gold rising above you.

Shigatse Dzong (Samdruptse) dominates the city from a hill. Rebuilt in the 2000s on the ruins of the original fortress destroyed during the Cultural Revolution, it's now partly a museum. The view over the Yarlung Tsangpo Valley and Tashilhunpo Monastery from its terraces is remarkable.

Lunch stop in Shigatse on the return from Rongbuk—3 hours drive. One last hour in the Tibetan market alleys behind Tashilhunpo, with yak butter traders and metal artisans, before taking the road back to Lhasa.

activities.text: Shigatse market is more authentic and less touristy than Lhasa's Barkhor. Tibetan merchants sell metal objects, textiles, yak butter in blocks, and spices. It's the circuit's best place to buy real souvenirs at reasonable prices.

Prepare for tomorrow's climb: Rongbuk and Everest Base Camp are 300km away, via Tingri, with passes to cross. Verify with your guide that Alien Travel Permits for the Everest zone are in order—they won't let you go without them.
Last souvenir shopping at Shigatse market—more authentic and less touristy than Barkhor. Good timing before the 3-hour drive to Lhasa eats up the afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days
From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Songpan, an ancient Ming-dynasty fortified town at 2,850 meters elevation, preserves three monumental gates and stone ramparts that straddle the Min River. The town was a strategic trading post on the tea route between Han China and Tibet—merchants exchanged tea, salt, horses, and furs here. The streets retain a Han, Tibetan, Qiang, and Hui mix.

The covered bridge at the North Gate spans the Min River between two watchtowers. The encircling walls, 6.2 km long, were built under Emperor Hongwu's reign (14th century) to protect this key position at the empire's edge.

activities.text: Browse Songpan's market: Tibetan medicinal herbs (including the famous caterpillar fungus—yartsa gunbu), wild honey, dried yak cheese, and Qiang products. The town serves as a base for treks to Huanglong and Jiuzhaigou.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

places.name: Suzhou

visits.text: The Humble Administrator's Garden (拙政园), built in 1509 during the Ming Dynasty by disgraced official Wang Xianchen, is Suzhou's largest classical garden - 5 hectares, two-thirds covered in water. Pavilions seem to float between ponds and lotus flowers. UNESCO-listed in 1997. Arrive at 7:30am to beat the crowds.

The neighboring Suzhou Museum, designed by I.M. Pei and opened in 2006, is itself a masterpiece. Born in Suzhou, Pei reinterpreted garden architecture in refined modernism: white walls, dark angles, geometric roofs. Collections of bronzes, ceramics, and Ming-Qing paintings. Free admission.

Lion Grove Garden (狮子林), built in 1342 as a retreat for Zen Buddhist monks, is famous for its labyrinth of Taihu rock formations - dozens of caves and passages that seem from another planet. Easy to get lost. The painter Ni Zan stayed here in the 14th century and created several scrolls depicting it.

Pingjiang Road (平江路) is the old town's best-preserved lane - one kilometer along Pingjiang Canal, white facades and gray tiled roofs, arched bridges, silk shops and cafes in interior courtyards. Come in late afternoon when the low-angle light plays on the water.

The Master of the Nets Garden (网师园), founded in 1180 during the Southern Song Dynasty, is Suzhou's smallest classical garden - less than half a hectare - and probably the most refined. Its designer Shi Zhengzhi multiplied depth effects so each meter seems doubled. A design masterpiece to be savored slowly.

Tiger Hill (虎丘) rises above the supposed tomb of King He Lü, founder of Suzhou in the 5th century BCE. At the 36-meter summit, Yunyan Pagoda (961 CE) leans 2.3 degrees - the Chinese call it "the Leaning Tower of the East." Come in the morning before the tour groups arrive.

The Lingering Garden (留园, Liuyuan) is one of China's four greatest gardens. Its distinctive feature: a 700-meter covered gallery connected by over 200 openings cut in varied shapes (vase, leaf, fan) that frame the garden like moving paintings.

Shantang Street (山塘街) runs 3.5 kilometers along the canal dredged by poet-governor Bai Juyi in the 9th century. Called "China's first ancient street" by local tradition, it lines up silk shops, shadow puppet theaters, and teahouses. In the evening, red lanterns on the water create the effect of an animated woodblock print.

activities.text: Visit a silk workshop: Suzhou was for centuries China's leading silk-producing city. Several factories offer tours with weaving demonstrations and double-sided embroidery - a technique visible from both sides with no reverse, a national specialty.
Dine on the lanes around Guanqian Street (观前街), Suzhou's gastronomic artery. Try: zhurou tongyuan (glutinous rice balls with pork), shumo (crab dumplings), eight-treasure rice soup.

Attend an evening kunqu opera performance in one of the gardens. Kunqu is China's oldest opera form, born in Suzhou in the 16th century - it preceded and influenced Beijing Opera. Intimate chamber performances in pavilions: intimate setting, natural acoustics.
Take an evening boat ride on Suzhou's canals from Shantang pier. The navigation passes illuminated facades and goes under stone bridges. Duration about 1 hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui
Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

map_keywords: Master of the Nets Garden Suzhou

visits.text: Tagong (塔公) sits on a plateau at 3,700m amid grasslands of an almost surreal green. Tagong Monastery dates to the 14th century and belongs to the Sakya school—it houses a replica of Lhasa's famous Jowo statue, offered according to legend by Princess Wencheng during her passage in the 7th century. A dozen white stupas encircle the main building. Monks come and go between buildings with cell phones in their pockets—21st-century Tibet.

From Tagong, on clear days you can see Mount Yala (雅拉神山), a sacred peak rising to 5,820m. For the region's nomads, it's the king of Kham's mountains—no climbing expeditions are permitted. The prayer flags covering the heights around the village are dedicated to it. Sit in the grasslands and look—you probably won't need to do anything else.

Xinduqiao (新都桥), 40 minutes from Tagong on the G318, is nicknamed photographers' paradise. The Tibetan village is surrounded by golden poplars (in autumn) and rolling hills. Stop for 30 minutes: the early afternoon light on the golden roofs and black stone houses is remarkable.

activities.text: Horseback ride through Tagong grasslands—local Khampa guides offer 1-3 hour outings on their plateau horses. It's the only way to truly feel the immensity of these pastures where yak herds graze as far as the eye can see.

Settle into a Tibetan guesthouse in Tagong for the night. Most are run by local families—the welcome is simple, the yak blankets thick. Step outside after dark: at 3,700m, far from any light pollution, the sky is dazzling.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Explore the Stone Fortress ruins at Tashkurgan, an ancient citadel perched at 3,200 meters altitude. This frontier outpost guarded the mountain passes connecting China to the kingdoms of Central Asia for over two millennia.

Discover Tashkurgan's Tajik community, an ethnic minority with Persian roots distinct from both Uyghurs and Han Chinese. Their colorful traditional dress, music, and hospitality reflect ancient connections to the Iranian world.

Drive the Panlong zigzag road, an engineering marvel with 600 hairpin turns ascending through dramatic high-altitude scenery near the Pakistan border-one of the world's most spectacular mountain drives.

activities.text: Visit a Tajik village to witness traditional eagle hunting demonstrations, a practice connecting these mountain people to nomadic cultures across the Eurasian steppes.

days_plan.itin_title: The Silk Road Overland: Xi'an to Istanbul via Uzbekistan & Turkmenistan

visits.text: Tingri (仲巴) is a town without particular interest, but it marks the entrance to the Everest zone. From surrounding passes, on clear days, you glimpse Everest's black pyramid (Qomolangma in Tibetan, "Goddess Mother of the World") and Cho Oyu. It's a mandatory stop for acclimatization before ascending to Rongbuk at 5,019m.

activities.text: Lunch in Tingri and observation of the Himalayan panorama from a restaurant terrace. It's the last stage before altitude exceeds 5,000m again. Drink water, sleep if possible during the break.

days_plan.itin_title: From Tibetan Kham to Everest: Chengdu, Yading & Lhasa in 18 Days

visits.text: Tongli (同里) is the region's most elegant water town. Tuisi Garden (退思园), UNESCO-listed in 2000, was designed in 1885 by Ren Lansheng, a disgraced official - its name means "reflecting while retiring." Unique feature: the buildings descend eastward following a progression toward the water, as if the entire composition were sliding into the pond.

Tongli's canals connect five islands - a configuration unique in the region. Three historic bridges (Taiping, Jili, Changqing) span the waters face-to-face: newlyweds would cross them in a precise order to ensure happiness, fortune, and longevity. The tradition lives on.

activities.text: Take a ride in a round-roofed boat through the canals - the navigation passes under bridges and alongside houses built directly on the water. The boatman paddles standing at the stern, traditional style.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Longwu (Rongwo) Monastery, founded in 1301, is the largest monastery in Rebkong and the third most important in the Amdo region. Originally of the Sakya tradition, it was later converted to the Gelugpa school. Around 400 monks reside here in a labyrinth of prayer halls with walls covered in murals and gilded statues.

The upper and lower Wutun villages, 10 km from Tongren, are the birthplace of Regong art—thangka painting, sculpture, embroidery, and architectural decoration inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list since 2009. Nearly every household is a workshop. Artists use mineral pigments (gold, silver, turquoise, malachite, saffron, indigo) that keep colors from fading for centuries.

The Regong Art Museum in Tongren (free admission) displays over 2,000 pieces from the 11th century to the present: thangkas, sculptures, embroideries. The collection traces the evolution of this art form, which blends Indian, Nepalese, and Chinese influences into a style unique to Amdo.

activities.text: Visit a thangka workshop in Wutun village. Master painters work with a two-hair brush for the finest details, following the strict proportions of the Buddhist "Lection of Measurement." A small thangka requires at least a month; large pieces, several years.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

places.name: Tunxi

visits.text: Tunxi's Old Street (老街) stretches 1.3 km along the Xin'an River: continuous Ming-Qing facades, antique shops, tea and traditional painting merchants. This is where collectors from all over China come to find paintings from the Xin'an school and Huizhou inkstones. A final easy stroll before the return flight from TXN airport (1h to Shanghai).

activities.text: Final stop at an Old Street teahouse to taste Keemun tea (祁门红茶) - Anhui's most renowned black tea, produced 60 km away in Qimen County. Also a good moment to buy Chinese ink, traditional brushes, or Xin'an school paintings.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

map_keywords: Old Street Laojie Tunxi Huangshan City

visits.text: The ancient city of Jiaohe (Jiāohé Gùchéng), 10 km west of Turpan, is the world's largest and best-preserved rammed-earth city. Perched on a 30-meter-high leaf-shaped loess plateau between two ravines of dried rivers, it served as a natural defense—no ramparts needed. Founded in the 2nd century BC as the capital of the Cheshi kingdom, it prospered as a Silk Road relay during the Tang dynasty (7th-10th century), when it housed the Protectorate of the Western Regions, the highest Chinese authority in Central Asia. The complex covers 370,000 m², with a central avenue, residential quarters, a palace, and a Buddhist temple complex. Destroyed by the Mongols in the 13th century, the site was preserved by Turpan's extremely dry climate. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2014.

The Karez irrigation system (Kǎnérjǐng), over 2,000 years old, is an underground canal network that channels meltwater from the Tian Shan Mountains to the Turpan oasis across the desert, without evaporation. The Turpan area has about 1,100 karez over a total network of roughly 5,000 km of tunnels—considered one of ancient China's three great engineering works along with the Great Wall and the Grand Canal. At a dedicated museum, you descend 10-50 meters underground to see the canals, access wells, and understand this gravity irrigation principle.

The Emin Minaret (Sūgōng Tǎ), 3 km east of Turpan, is China's tallest Islamic tower at 44 meters. Built in 1777 from raw clay bricks, its conical shaft is decorated with 15 different geometric patterns forming diamonds, waves, and flowers—all in earth, without a single nail or piece of wood.

The Flaming Mountains (Huǒyàn Shān), 30 km east of Turpan, are a 100-km-long chain of eroded red sandstone whose gullies and striations give the illusion of flames under the sun. Famous throughout China thanks to the classic 16th-century novel Journey to the West, where the Monkey King Sun Wukong crosses them with a magic fan. Ground temperature can exceed 80°C in summer—Turpan, in a depression 154 meters below sea level, is China's hottest city.

The Tuyok Valley (Tǔyùgōu), nestled in a Flaming Mountains gorge, shelters a traditional Uyghur pisé village where time seems suspended. Raw clay brick houses, grape trellises drying on rooftops, and winding alleys compose an intact landscape. Above the village, Buddhist caves carved between the 5th and 9th centuries preserve fresco fragments. The site is also a Muslim pilgrimage destination—some consider it a "little Mecca."

The Bezeklik Thousand Buddha Caves (Bǎizīkèlǐkè), in a canyon at the foot of the Flaming Mountains, number 77 caves carved between the 5th and 14th centuries. Their frescoes blend Indian Buddhist, Chinese, and Uyghur influences. Many were damaged by Muslim iconoclasts in the 15th century, then cut out and carried off by European explorers in the early 20th century—fragments now reside in Berlin's museum and St. Petersburg's Hermitage.

activities.text: Day 1: Drive through the legendary Flaming Mountains, spectacular red sandstone formations stretching over 100 km—China's hottest place and setting of a famous Chinese legend featuring a monkey king and a wise monk.
Day 2: Hike through the Flaming Mountains gorges from Tuyok, a challenging 5-hour trek crossing the red sandstone massif to emerge into landscapes of extraordinary geological drama.

China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: The Xinjiang Regional Museum in Urumqi displays the Tarim Basin mummies, 3,800 years old, including the famous "Beauty of Loulan" with her Caucasian features—proof of exchanges between eastern and western populations long before the Silk Road's official opening. Collections cover the cultures of Xinjiang's 13 ethnic groups (Uyghurs, Kazakhs, Kyrgyz, Mongols, etc.) through textiles, musical instruments, and everyday objects.

Heavenly Lake (Tiānchí), 115 km east of Urumqi in the Tian Shan Mountains, sits at 1,910 meters altitude. This 3.4-km-long, 1.5-km-wide glacial lake is framed by spruce forests and dominated by Bogda Peak, which rises to 5,445 meters. Kazakh herders pitch their yurts here in summer. The site, important to Taoism and Buddhism, is listed as UNESCO World Heritage as part of the Tian Shan network.

Red Hill (Hóngshān), a small park in Urumqi's center, offers views over the city and the Tian Shan Mountains in the background. At the summit, a 10.6-meter red brick pagoda built in 1788 during the Qing dynasty was meant to prevent the mythical dragon of the Urumqi River from causing floods.

activities.text: Stroll through the Erdaoqiao International Grand Bazaar, Central Asian China's largest covered market. You'll find carpets, Uyghur knives, dried fruits (Turpan raisins, walnuts, apricots), spices, and musical instruments. Food stalls serve laghman (Uyghur hand-pulled noodles), samsa (meat pastries), and nan (tandoor-baked bread).

Final stop at Urumqi's dried fruit and spice market. Xinjiang produces China's finest raisins, dried apricots, walnuts, and figs—the region's dry, sunny climate gives them high sugar concentration. Free time before departure flight.

days_plan.itin_title: China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor
China's Silk Road: Xi'an to Kashgar via the Hexi Corridor

visits.text: Wuhu (芜湖) marks the gateway to deep Anhui on the southern bank of the Yangtze. Guangji Temple (广济寺), founded in 894 during the Tang Dynasty, is nicknamed "Little Jiuhua" - its 88 steps lead to the halls of Maitreya, the Great Pagoda, and the Medicine Buddha. River view from the hill. A brief but solid stop between Jiangsu's China and Anhui's mountains.

Zheshan Park (赭山公园) is Wuhu's emblematic hill, overlooking the Yangtze. The trails that circle it pass historical remnants and offer a panorama of the river and city. Pleasant for a 45-minute walk between drives.

activities.text: Lunch on the Yangtze banks in the Jinghu district - fresh freshwater fish, a regional specialty. Wuhu has been nicknamed "the city of fish and rice" since antiquity.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Tianyou Peak ("Heavenly Tour") is Wuyi Mountain's iconic summit: 848 steps carved into the cliff lead to a viewpoint above the 5th and 6th bends of the Nine Bend River. The panorama over red Danxia sandstone peaks crowned with subtropical vegetation justifies the climb.

The Da Hong Pao area shelters the six mother bushes of this legendary oolong tea, clinging to the cliff of Jiulongke ravine. These plants, estimated at over 350 years old, are World Heritage listed. Since 2006, all harvesting from these mother plants has been prohibited. The Tea Trail (4.5 km) descends through rock walls where hundreds of varieties of rock tea (yancha) grow to Water Curtain Cave.

Thread of Sky (Yixiantian) is a passage between two walls over 50 meters high, so narrow that the tightest point measures only 30 centimeters. You progress sideways, glimpsing a thin line of sky above your head.

Tiger Roaring Rock (Huxiao Yan) owes its name to wind rushing into a giant cavity in the cliff. The trail climbs steeply along the wall via stairs clinging to the rock, with plunging views over the subtropical forest.

Wuyi Palace, at the mountain's foot, was an 11th-century Neo-Confucian center. Philosopher Zhu Xi developed thought here that would influence all of East Asia for centuries. The UNESCO site covers 999 km² and includes ruins of a 1st-century BC Han city.

activities.text: Bamboo raft ride down the Nine Bend River, 9.5 km in about 2 hours. The raft passes between 200 to 400-meter red sandstone cliffs. The raftsman comments on rock formations and legends. The river owes its name to its nine sharp turns between the peaks.

Tea tasting at a teahouse at the foot of Wuyi Mountain. Tea masters prepare Da Hong Pao using the gongfu method: Yixing clay teapot, water at 95-100°C, successive infusions of 10 to 15 seconds. The aromas evolve from infusion to infusion—roasted notes first, then floral and fruity. Wuyi produces over 300 varieties of rock tea.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

places.name: Wuzhen

visits.text: Wuzhen West (西栅, Xizha) is the most successful part of the water town: renovated in the 2000s, it has preserved rare architectural coherence - gray wooden houses, black tiles, covered galleries above the water. In the evening, red lanterns light up along the entire length of the main canal and the water surface becomes a mirror. This is the hour not to miss - ticket valid until 10:30pm.

Writer Mao Dun's house (茅盾故居), in the East Quarter, is the birthplace of the revolutionary novelist (1896-1981), author of Midnight. Opened to the public in 1985, the rooms preserve period furniture - a window into early 20th-century bourgeois Jiangnan life.

Wuzhen's indigo dyeing workshop is one of the region's oldest. Hundreds of indigo fabric squares dry hanging in garlands above the East Quarter lanes - an unmissable photographic image. The artisan demonstrates the tie-resist process before vat dyeing.

activities.text: Take a covered boat (乌篷船) on Wuzhen's canals in late afternoon to navigate between the houses before sunset. Depart from Xizha's central pier, less crowded than the main dock.
Watch the shadow puppet show (皮影戏) and opera performance in the East Quarter - 20 to 30-minute shows, repeated several times throughout the day. Schedules are posted at the entrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

map_keywords: Wuzhen Xizha West Quarter night lanterns

visits.text: Labrang Monastery, established in 1709, ranks among the six great monasteries of the Gelugpa school (Yellow Hats) of Tibetan Buddhism and stands as the largest outside the Tibet Autonomous Region. Around 1,600 monks study here across six colleges covering philosophy, medicine, astronomy, and tantra. The Grand Prayer Hall, supported by 140 massive pillars, accommodates up to 3,000 monks for collective chanting. At its early 20th-century peak, Labrang housed nearly 4,000 monks.

The Labrang kora—the circumambulation circuit—stretches 3.5 km around the monastery, lined with over 1,700 hexagonal painted prayer wheels. This is the world's longest prayer wheel corridor. Tibetan pilgrims walk it clockwise from dawn, spinning each wheel as they pass. A complete circuit takes about an hour.

The golden Gongtang Stupa, within the monastery complex, houses relics of a revered lama. Its gilded roof gleams in the sunlight above the monastery's whitewashed roofs. From the hill behind, the panorama embraces the entire monastery, the Daxia River, and the surrounding grasslands.

Sangke Prairie, 14 km from Xiahe, unfolds at the foot of rolling hills at 3,000 meters elevation. Tibetan nomadic families graze yaks and sheep here. In summer, wildflowers paint the steppe in color and yurt camps welcome overnight visitors.

Ganjia Prairie, quieter than Sangke, preserves the ruins of the octagonal Bajiao fortress, a rammed-earth citadel visible from the road. Nomads here live in black yak-hair tents, raising horses and sheep as their ancestors did.

activities.text: Hike up the hill south of the monastery for a sweeping view over Labrang, the golden rooftops, and the Daxia River valley. The trail starts behind the white stupa to the left of the complex and takes roughly 45 minutes uphill.

If your visit coincides with a monastic debate day at Labrang (typically afternoons), monks square off in the courtyard, clapping their hands to punctuate philosophical arguments—a striking auditory and visual spectacle.
Sample yak yogurt at the shops along Xiahe's main street. The local yogurt, thick as custard and slightly tangy, is served in ceramic bowls with sugar or honey.

days_plan.itin_title: Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou
Hidden China: Tibetan Gansu, Qinghai & Jiuzhaigou

visits.text: Gulangyu Island, 600 meters from Xiamen's shore, has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2017. The 2 km² island was an international concession from 1903 to 1943, explaining its 1,000 buildings mixing colonial, Art Deco, and traditional Chinese styles. Cars are prohibited. The island once counted up to 500 pianos in the early 20th century, earning it the nickname "Music Island."

Nanputuo Temple, at the foot of Mount Wulao, is a Buddhist monastery founded during the Tang dynasty and rebuilt under the Qing. Dedicated to Guanyin, it serves an active monastic community and houses a Buddhist school. The four main halls climb up the hillside.
Sunlight Rock (Riguang Yan) is Gulangyu's highest point at 93 meters. At the summit, a 360° panorama embraces the island, the Taiwan Strait, and Fujian's mountains.

Shuzhuang Garden, created in 1913 by an exiled Taiwanese scholar, integrates the sea into its landscape composition—coastal rocks are part of the garden. The Piano Museum, within the grounds, displays over 100 antique pianos from Europe and America, from the 17th to the 20th century.

Zhongshan Road, Xiamen's main shopping artery, stretches 1.2 km under 1920s-1930s colonial-style arcades (qilou). These covered galleries, typical of southern Fujian and influenced by Southeast Asian architecture, blend European and Chinese elements across three to five stories.

activities.text: Ferry crossing to Gulangyu from Dongdu terminal (20 minutes). Free stroll through pedestrian alleys, between colonial facades covered with bougainvillea and local pastry shops.
Seafood tasting at Xiamen harbor market. Fujian is renowned for oyster omelets (haolicuijian), clam vermicelli, and crab spring rolls. The market bustles in late afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian
Minority China: From Guizhou to Fujian

visits.text: Flight from Beijing to Xi'an (about 2 hours). The Terracotta Army, discovered on March 29, 1974, by farmers digging a well, lies 19 miles east of Xi'an. Commissioned by Emperor Qin Shi Huang—who unified China in 221 BC and launched construction of the Great Wall—this funerary army was meant to protect him in the afterlife. Pit 1, the largest (755 feet × 203 feet), contains over 6,000 warriors and horses arranged in a rectangular battle formation across 11 corridors. Each statue, standing 5.9 to 6.6 feet tall, has a unique face: expressions, hairstyles, armor, and ranks differ from soldier to soldier. The statues were fired at 1,650°F in kilns, then painted with mineral pigments to distinguish units. In total, excavations have uncovered about 8,000 warriors, 130 chariots, 520 horses, and over 10,000 bronze weapons. The site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987.

Pit 2, to the northeast, contains about 1,000 warriors with greater variety: crossbowmen, infantry, cavalry, and war chariots in a combined unit. Display cases let you observe details up close—seven variants of Qin armor have been identified. Pit 3, the smallest (5,380 square feet), U-shaped, holds 68 high-ranking officers and a ceremonial chariot drawn by four horses: this is the underground army's headquarters. The emperor's tomb itself, covered by a 377-foot-high mound, has never been opened—archaeologists fear the artifacts would degrade upon contact with air, as happened with the first warriors' paint when they were unearthed.

The Xi'an City Wall, built between 1374 and 1378 under the first Ming emperor Zhu Yuanzhang, forms a rectangle with an 8.5-mile perimeter around the old town. First constructed of rammed earth mixed with lime and glutinous rice juice, then covered with bricks in 1568, it rises 39 feet high, 49 to 59 feet wide at the base, and 39 to 46 feet wide at the top. A moat 66 feet wide and 33 feet deep encircles the wall. Four monumental gates pierce the ramparts at the cardinal points; 98 watchtowers, spaced 394 feet apart—a distance calculated based on half an arrow's range—line the route. Enter through the South Gate (Yongning), the most ornately decorated, whose main tower rises 105 feet.

The Muslim Quarter (Huimin Jie), at the foot of the Drum Tower, has been home to Xi'an's Hui community since the Tang dynasty (7th century), when Xi'an—then called Chang'an—was the starting point of the Silk Road. The alleys overflow with stalls selling hand-pulled noodles (biang biang mian), grilled lamb skewers, and flatbreads (roujiamo). The Great Mosque, founded in 742 during the Tang dynasty and rebuilt in the 14th century, blends traditional Chinese architecture with Islamic elements across a 129,000-square-foot garden.

The Giant Wild Goose Pagoda (Dayan Ta), built in 652 during the Tang dynasty to house Buddhist scriptures brought from India by the monk Xuanzang after 17 years of travel. The current pagoda, renovated under Empress Wu Zetian, has 7 stories and stands 210 feet tall. It's part of an ensemble inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2014 as part of the Silk Road corridor sites.

activities.text: Wander the alleys and sample local specialties
Sunset over the old town from the city walls
Visit a cave restaurant and learn to cook spicy local dishes
Watch the fountain show at the pagoda in the evening
High-speed train Xi'an → Beijing
Free stroll through downtown shopping streets

Explore Tang Paradise and Furong Lake, a vast theme park southeast of the Giant Wild Goose Pagoda. This recreated Tang dynasty garden offers lakeside walks and nightly water shows celebrating China's golden age.
Explore Xi'an's Muslim Quarter and its traditional markets.
Walk through the historic center and visit the Bell Tower and Drum Tower.

Bike the city walls: rentals are available at each gate. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours to complete the 8.5-mile circuit, with contrasting views of the old town inside and modern towers outside. The walkway, 39 to 46 feet wide, is perfectly flat.
Tasting in the Muslim Quarter: biang biang noodles hand-pulled before your eyes, cumin lamb skewers, pao mo lamb soup (bread crumbled into broth), sesame cakes, and fresh-pressed pomegranate juice.

visits.text: Xidi (西递), founded during the Song Dynasty and developed by the Hu family from the 14th to 18th centuries, is Anhui's best-preserved Hui village. The Qingyi Memorial Arch (1578), carved on three levels, celebrates a Hu official's success in the imperial examinations. Behind it, 124 Ming-Qing residences organize along lanes paved with blue flagstones. UNESCO-listed in 2000.

Xidi is quieter and less crowded than Hongcun - tour groups favor the Moon Pond. Get lost in the side lanes behind the merchant houses: ochre rammed-earth walls pierced with geometric windows, dead-ends closed by raw wood doors. The atmosphere of a village still alive.

activities.text: Look for a Chinese ink or calligraphy workshop in Xidi's lanes. Huizhou ink has been produced in the region for 2,000 years and remains the reference for traditional calligraphers nationwide.

days_plan.itin_title: Cultural Eastern China - 18 Days Through Jiangnan & Anhui

visits.text: Shiqiao Miao Village, in Danzhai County, is the last place in China where paper is made by hand using Tang dynasty (7th century) techniques. More than 60 families still practice this millennial craft: local tree bark soaked, boiled in lime, beaten into pulp, then spread on a bamboo screen. The resulting paper can be preserved for 1,500 years—the National Library and National Museum of China use it to restore ancient books and paintings. The process, inscribed on the national intangible heritage list in 2006, involves more than ten steps.

Chuandong Cave, adjacent to Shiqiao village, houses an underground paper mill fed by a spring whose slightly alkaline water is considered ideal for papermaking. Stalactites grow at an unusual rate here, giving the walls a soft texture to the touch.

Xijiang, the "Thousand Household Village," is the world's largest Miao village: 1,432 families, over 6,000 inhabitants of whom 99.5% are Miao. Wooden fir stilted houses climb in tiers on both sides of the Baishui River, linked by Wind and Rain Bridges. The village is divided into ten natural hamlets at the foot of Mount Leigong.

The Miao Nationality Museum, in the village, traces the history of this people through costumes, orally transmitted "old songs," and agricultural tools. Special emphasis is placed on silver jewelry making—hammering, filigree, granulation—practiced by village silversmiths for generations.

activities.text: Flower paper (huacao) workshop in Shiqiao: visitors dip a screen into bark pulp, place dried flowers and leaves on the wet layer, then add a second layer of pulp. Drying takes a week—the paper is then shipped.

Kaili Sunday Market gathers Miao villagers from the surrounding area every Sunday. Embroidered textiles, hammered silver jewelry, medicinal herbs, and sour fish soup (suantangyu) mingle in a rural fair atmosphere.
Climb to Xijiang viewpoint in late afternoon: at dusk, thousands of lights illuminate the valley, transforming the stilted houses into a luminous amphitheater.

Miao song and dance performance on the central square (around 11:30 am and 5:00 pm). The "Miao old songs" are polyphonic vocals without choir leader or instrumental accompaniment, transmitted by word of mouth for centuries.

Long table banquet (changzhuo yan): guests sit around a table several dozen meters long. Pickled sour fish, salted vegetables, and chili-boiled chicken, accompanied by rice wine served in buffalo horns.
